abstract: Large language models are increasingly post-trained with reinforcement learning in verifiable domains such as code and math. Yet, current methods for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) learn only from a scalar outcome reward per attempt, creating a severe credit-assignment bottleneck. Many verifiable environments actually provide rich textual feedback, such as runtime errors or judge evaluations, that explain why an attempt failed. We formalize this setting as reinforcement learning with rich feedback and introduce Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO), which converts tokenized feedback into a dense learning signal without any external teacher or explicit reward model. SDPO treats the current model conditioned on feedback as a self-teacher and distills its feedback-informed next-token predictions back into the policy. In this way, SDPO leverages the model's ability to retrospectively identify its own mistakes in-context. Across scientific reasoning, tool use, and competitive programming on LiveCodeBench v6, SDPO improves sample efficiency and final accuracy over strong RLVR baselines. Notably, SDPO also outperforms baselines in standard RLVR environments that only return scalar feedback by using successful rollouts as implicit feedback for failed attempts. Finally, applying SDPO to individual questions at test time accelerates discovery on difficult binary-reward tasks, achieving the same discovery probability as best-of-k sampling or multi-turn conversations with 3x fewer attempts.

keyInsights: Large Language Models (LLMs) possess an inherent ability for in-context learning, allowing them to effectively self-correct and provide dense learning signals when re-prompted with their own failures and rich feedback.

Dense, token-level credit assignment is critical for improving both learning efficiency and the quality of reasoning in LLMs, leading to more concise and accurate responses than sequence-level rewards.

SDPO, as an on-policy self-distillation method, allows for continuous policy refinement and can accelerate the discovery of solutions for challenging problems, while effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting of prior capabilities.
originalProblem: Current Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods suffer from a
credit-assignment bottleneck,
as sparse scalar rewards make it hard to identify specific errors in long generated sequences.
Many verifiable environments provide rich textual feedback (e.g., runtime errors, unit test failures) that is often underutilized by traditional RL methods.
Generating dense supervision typically requires external, stronger teacher models or expensive human labeling, which are often unavailable in online learning settings.
results: SDPO consistently outperformed GRPO baselines, achieving a 10x speedup in wall-clock training time and higher final accuracy (e.g., 76.8% vs. 54.3% on Chemistry) while producing over 3x shorter and more concise responses.
On competitive programming tasks, SDPO reached higher final accuracy (48.8% vs. 41.2%) and did so with 4x fewer generations than GRPO, also surpassing top commercial instruct models.

For hard, sparse-reward questions, SDPO significantly accelerated solution discovery, achieving a 53.2% discovery probability with approximately 3x fewer generations than best-of-k sampling and solving questions unsolveable by baselines.
solution: The paper formalizes Reinforcement Learning with Rich Feedback (RLRF) to explicitly leverage detailed, tokenized feedback from verifiable environments.
Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO) is introduced, an on-policy algorithm that uses the current LLM itself as a
self-teacher,
to provide dense, logit-level supervision when conditioned on rich feedback.
SDPO converts rich textual feedback into precise, token-level advantages for each possible next token, offering fine-grained credit assignment unlike sparse scalar rewards.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from ETH Zurich, Max Planck Institute, MIT, and Stanford developed Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO), an on-policy algorithm that trains large language models by transforming rich environmental feedback into dense, token-level learning signals. This method yields a 10x speedup in training time, requires 4x fewer generations to reach baseline accuracy on competitive programming tasks, and enables solution discovery for hard problems with 3x fewer generations.

title: Reinforcement Learning via Self-Distillation

abstract: Knowledge distillation improves large language model (LLM) reasoning by compressing the knowledge of a teacher LLM to train smaller LLMs. On-policy distillation advances this approach by having the student sample its own trajectories while a teacher LLM provides dense token-level supervision, addressing the distribution mismatch between training and inference in off-policy distillation methods. However, on-policy distillation typically requires a separate, often larger, teacher LLM and does not explicitly leverage ground-truth solutions available in reasoning datasets. Inspired by the intuition that a sufficiently capable LLM can rationalize external privileged reasoning traces and teach its weaker self, we introduce On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), a learning algorithm where a single LLM acts as both teacher and student with different contexts. The teacher policy conditions on privileged information (e.g., verified reasoning traces) while the student policy sees only the question; training minimizes the per-token divergence between these distributions over the student's own rollouts. We demonstrate the efficacy of our method on multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks, achieving superior token efficiency compared to reinforcement learning methods and better performance over off-policy distillation methods. Code repo: this https URL.

keyInsights: A single large language model can effectively self-distill and improve its reasoning capabilities by leveraging ground-truth solutions to guide its 'weaker self' through rationalization.
Providing dense, token-level feedback on the student's own generated trajectories is highly token-efficient and reduces distribution mismatch compared to off-policy methods.
The effectiveness of self-distillation is influenced by model capacity, with larger models (4B, 8B parameters) showing more pronounced performance improvements.
originalProblem: Existing on-policy distillation methods require a separate, often larger, teacher LLM, incurring computational and logistical overhead.
Reinforcement learning (e.g., GRPO) suffers from high computational cost due to multiple response samples and sparse, sequence-level reward signals.
Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on fixed expert trajectories is prone to exposure bias and can exhibit weaker generalization.
results: OPSD matched or surpassed GRPO's performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks for Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B models, achieving 52.2% average accuracy for Qwen3-8B compared to GRPO's 51.3%.
The method demonstrated 8-12x higher token efficiency than GRPO, reaching comparable performance using only one rollout per problem versus GRPO's eight.
Full-vocabulary logit distillation proved more effective than sampled-token distillation, and increasing the generation length of student responses positively impacted performance.
solution: A single LLM is configured to act as both a teacher (conditioned on problem + ground-truth solution) and a student (conditioned only on the problem).

The student policy generates on-policy responses, and the training objective minimizes the token-wise divergence between the teacher's and student's next-token distributions on these self-generated trajectories.
The approach leverages ground-truth solutions as privileged information to provide dense, token-level supervision, addressing sparsity issues found in sparse reward signals.
paper_summary.summary: On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) enables a single large language model to enhance its reasoning abilities by serving as both teacher and student, providing dense, token-level supervision on student-generated trajectories informed by ground-truth solutions. This method achieves performance comparable to or exceeding GRPO on mathematical reasoning tasks, while demonstrating 8-12x greater token efficiency.

title: Self-Distilled Reasoner: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Large Language Models

abstract: Context distillation enables language models to internalize in-context knowledge into their parameters. In our work, we propose On-Policy Context Distillation (OPCD), a framework that bridges on-policy distillation with context distillation by training a student model on its own generated trajectories while minimizing reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence against a context-conditioned teacher. We demonstrate the effectiveness of OPCD on two important applications: experiential knowledge distillation, where models extract and consolidate transferable knowledge from their historical solution traces, and system prompt distillation, where models internalize beneficial behaviors encoded in optimized prompts. Across mathematical reasoning, text-based games, and domain-specific tasks, OPCD consistently outperforms baseline methods, achieving higher task accuracy while better preserving out-of-distribution capabilities. We further show that OPCD enables effective cross-size distillation, where smaller student models can internalize experiential knowledge from larger teachers.

keyInsights: On-policy training with reverse KL divergence effectively mitigates exposure bias and mode-covering issues prevalent in prior off-policy distillation techniques.
Explicitly extracting and structuring 'experiential knowledge' from raw solution traces is crucial for effective knowledge transfer, as simply prepending raw traces can degrade performance.

Teacher-student distillation, where a frozen teacher guides the student, significantly outperforms self-distillation due to more stable training dynamics and reduced variance from an evolving teacher.
originalProblem: In-context knowledge in Large Language Models (LLMs) is temporary, requiring repeated provision of prompts or retrieved documents for each interaction.

Existing context distillation methods suffer from exposure bias due to off-policy training and forward Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, often leading to mode-covering behavior and potential hallucinations.
Valuable experiential knowledge from generated solutions or system prompt behaviors is not permanently integrated into the model's parameters, limiting self-improvement and efficiency.
results: OPCD consistently achieved higher in-distribution accuracy across diverse tasks, such as 79.7% for math problems with Qwen3-8B (vs. 78.5% for the baseline) and 26.5% for Frozen Lake with Qwen3-1.7B (vs. 22.9%).
The method better preserved out-of-distribution (OOD) capabilities, demonstrating higher IF-Eval scores (e.g., ~2% improvement on Frozen Lake) compared to off-policy context distillation.
OPCD effectively enabled smaller student models (e.g., Qwen3-1.7B, 4B) to internalize complex experiential knowledge from larger teacher models (Qwen3-8B), facilitating efficient model deployment.
solution: On-Policy Context Distillation (OPCD) trains a context-free student model to mimic a context-conditioned teacher model's behavior, internalizing the context into the student's parameters.
The method employs an on-policy sampling mechanism where the student generates its own complete response trajectories without explicit context during training, addressing exposure bias.
It minimizes reverse Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, which encourages mode-seeking behavior, guiding the student to focus on high-likelihood regions of the teacher's context-aware distribution.
paper_summary.summary: On-Policy Context Distillation (OPCD) allows language models to internalize transient in-context knowledge into their permanent parameters using an on-policy training strategy and reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence. This method consistently outperforms off-policy context distillation baselines, achieving, for instance, 79.7% accuracy on a math task compared to 78.5% for the baseline, and better preserves out-of-distribution capabilities.

title: On-Policy Context Distillation for Language Models

abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) is an emerging paradigm for improving the reasoning ability of large language models. However, standard on-policy training discards rollout experiences after a single update, leading to computational inefficiency and instability. While prior work on RL has highlighted the benefits of reusing past experience, the role of experience characteristics in shaping learning dynamics of large reasoning models remains underexplored. In this paper, we are the first to investigate what makes a reasoning experience valuable and identify rollout correctness and entropy as effective indicators of experience value. Based on these insights, we propose ExGRPO (Experiential Group Relative Policy Optimization), a framework that organizes and prioritizes valuable experiences, and employs a mixed-policy objective to balance exploration with experience exploitation. Experiments on five backbone models (1.5B-8B parameters) show that ExGRPO consistently improves reasoning performance on mathematical/general benchmarks, with an average gain of +3.5/7.6 points over on-policy RLVR. Moreover, ExGRPO stabilizes training on both stronger and weaker models where on-policy methods fail. These results highlight principled experience management as a key ingredient for efficient and scalable RLVR.

keyInsights: Reasoning experiences from questions of "medium" difficulty (25% to 75% correctness rate) provide the most effective learning signals for RLVR, outperforming very easy or very hard tasks.
Lower average token-level entropy within a reasoning trajectory correlates with higher logical correctness of the Chain-of-Thought (CoT), serving as a reliable online proxy for quality.

Careful management and selection of past experiences can prevent "misguided learning" from low-quality rollouts and significantly stabilize and improve LLM training, especially for weaker base models.
originalProblem: Current Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods for large language models (LLMs) rely on on-policy training, discarding valuable generated experiences after a single gradient update.
This practice leads to computational inefficiency and misses opportunities for the model to learn from past successful reasoning explorations.
The specific characteristics defining "valuable" reasoning experiences for LLM-based RLVR and their systematic exploitation had not been thoroughly investigated.
results: ExGRPO consistently improved mathematical reasoning performance, yielding an average gain of +3.5 points on in-distribution and +7.6 points on out-of-distribution benchmarks for Qwen2.5-Math 7B compared to on-policy RLVR baselines.
The framework stabilized RLVR training for weaker models like Llama-3.1 8B Base, preventing catastrophic collapse observed with on-policy methods and enabling successful learning.

Both the difficulty-based question sampling and entropy-based trajectory selection components empirically contributed to performance gains, with an optimal replay proportion of 50% experiential samples identified.
solution: ExGRPO introduces structured experience management, collecting successful reasoning trajectories and organizing them in a replay buffer based on question correctness rates.
It strategically selects experiences for replay by prioritizing questions of intermediate difficulty and trajectories exhibiting the lowest token-level entropy under the current policy.
A unified optimization objective combines on-policy samples with importance-sampled replayed experiences, integrating policy shaping and a delayed start mechanism for stable learning.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from University of Macau and Shanghai AI Laboratory developed ExGRPO, a framework that enhances large language model reasoning by strategically replaying past successful experiences in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). ExGRPO improved performance by 3.5 to 7.6 points on reasoning benchmarks and stabilized training for weaker models by prioritizing experiences of intermediate difficulty and low-entropy reasoning chains.

title: ExGRPO: Learning to Reason from Experience

abstract: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) are two prominent post-training paradigms for refining the capabilities and aligning the behavior of Large Language Models (LLMs). Existing approaches that integrate SFT and RL often face the risk of disrupting established response patterns and inducing overfitting to expert data. To address this, we present a novel investigation into the unified view of SFT and RL through an off-policy versus on-policy lens. We propose CHORD, a framework for Controllable Harmonization of On- and Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning via Dynamic Weighting, which reframes SFT not as a separate stage but as a dynamically weighted auxiliary objective within the on-policy RL process. Based on an analysis of off-policy expert data's influence at both holistic and granular levels, we incorporate a dual-control mechanism in CHORD. Specifically, the framework first employs a global coefficient to holistically guide the transition from off-policy imitation to on-policy exploration, and then applies a token-wise weighting function that enables granular learning from the expert, which promotes on-policy exploration and mitigates disruption from off-policy data. We conduct extensive experiments across various practical tasks, providing empirical evidence that CHORD achieves a stable and efficient learning process. By effectively harmonizing off-policy expert data with on-policy exploration, CHORD demonstrates significant improvements over baselines. We release the implementation at this https URL to inspire further research.

keyInsights: The observed "shift-readapt-overfit" progression highlights a fundamental instability in integrating off-policy expert data into LLMs with existing strong policies.
Dynamically controlled integration of off-policy data, through both global and fine-grained mechanisms, is crucial for stable learning and avoiding policy disruption or entropy collapse in LLMs.

Weighting SFT signals based on the model's uncertainty for a given expert token effectively creates a "learning sweet spot," promoting the integration of novel yet not excessively divergent expert knowledge.
originalProblem: Sequential Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)-then-Reinforcement Learning (RL) pipelines often yield suboptimal performance compared to pure RL, indicating issues with integrating these paradigms.

Applying SFT to instruction-tuned Large Language Models (LLMs) with established policies leads to a "shift-readapt-overfit" progression, causing initial performance degradation and eventual overfitting to limited expert data.

Existing integration strategies struggle with the distributional shift between an LLM's established policy and off-policy expert data, leading to instability and hindering effective knowledge transfer.
results: CHORD-ϕ consistently achieved the highest performance across mathematical reasoning (e.g., +4.1 on AMC, +1.1 on AIME24) and tool-use benchmarks (78.5% BFCL), outperforming conventional SFT-then-RL and other baselines.
The framework maintained balanced policy entropy, prevented premature collapse, and showed stable, continuous reward increases, demonstrating a more robust and efficient learning process.

CHORD proved effective and robust across various settings, including different expert data sources, non-verifiable domains, weaker policy models, and diverse model architectures like Mixture-of-Experts, indicating broad applicability.
solution: CHORD (Controllable Harmonization of On- and Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning via Dynamic Weighting) reconfigures SFT as a dynamically weighted auxiliary objective within the continuous on-policy RL process, rather than a separate sequential stage.

A global coefficient (μ) with a dynamic decay schedule is introduced to holistically manage the trade-off between RL and SFT objectives, smoothly transitioning the learning focus from off-policy imitation to on-policy exploration.

A token-wise weighting function ($\phi(y^*_t; \pi_\theta) = p_t(1 - p_t)$) is applied to the SFT loss, selectively down-weighting learning signals for expert tokens that are either highly probable (to prevent overconfidence) or extremely improbable (to avoid disruptive policy shifts).
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Alibaba Group developed CHORD, a framework that unifies Supervised Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning for Large Language Models through dynamic weighting, employing both global and token-wise control. This approach facilitates stable and efficient learning, leading to superior performance on mathematical reasoning and tool-use tasks compared to sequential training methods.

title: On-Policy RL Meets Off-Policy Experts: Harmonizing Supervised Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning via Dynamic Weighting

abstract: The success of RL for LLM post-training stems from an unreasonably uninformative source: a single bit of information per rollout as binary reward or preference label. At the other extreme, distillation offers dense supervision but requires demonstrations, which are costly and difficult to scale. We study text feedback as an intermediate signal: richer than scalar rewards, yet cheaper than complete demonstrations. Textual feedback is a natural mode of human interaction and is already abundant in many real-world settings, where users, annotators, and automated judges routinely critique LLM outputs. Towards leveraging text feedback at scale, we formalize a multi-turn RL setup, RL from Text Feedback (RLTF), where text feedback is available during training but not at inference. Therefore, models must learn to internalize the feedback in order to improve their test-time single-turn performance. To do this, we propose two methods: Self Distillation (RLTF-SD), which trains the single-turn policy to match its own feedback-conditioned second-turn generations; and Feedback Modeling (RLTF-FM), which predicts the feedback as an auxiliary objective. We provide theoretical analysis on both methods, and empirically evaluate on reasoning puzzles, competition math, and creative writing tasks. Our results show that both methods consistently outperform strong baselines across benchmarks, highlighting the potential of RL with an additional source of rich supervision at scale.

keyInsights: Semantically rich natural language critiques are crucial for effective LLM learning and internalization, providing specific, actionable information that scalar signals cannot convey.
Standard multi-turn RL approaches do not inherently lead to improved single-turn performance, underscoring the necessity of explicit mechanisms for feedback internalization.

In LLM output sequence distillation, trading a small amount of bias for significantly reduced variance (e.g., using an AWR-style approach without full importance weighting) enhances training stability and final performance.

Using a first-turn mean reward baseline in self-distillation prevents 'gradient-signal collapse' and ensures continuous learning for the initial policy, even when subsequent attempts become consistently successful.

Training an LLM to predict textual feedback (RLTF-FM) provides a dense supervised signal that helps learn representation directions weakly identified by sparse rewards, acting as a 'representation preconditioner'.
originalProblem: Existing reinforcement learning methods for large language models (LLMs) rely on sparse scalar rewards, which are inefficient and uninformative for complex tasks.
Collecting dense, token-level expert demonstrations for LLM fine-tuning is prohibitively costly and does not scale to diverse, frontier models.
Multi-turn reinforcement learning often fails to transfer feedback-conditioned competence into improved single-turn test-time performance, as feedback is typically unavailable during inference.
Reliance on scalar rewards leads to weak identifiability of representation directions in the model, making learning inefficient.
results: RLTF-SD and RLTF-FM consistently and significantly outperform baselines (GRPO single-turn/multi-turn, Feedback Descent) in single-turn test-time performance across diverse tasks like reasoning, math, and creative writing.
Ablation studies demonstrate that the AWR-style distillation objective and the proposed first-turn mean reward baseline are critical for RLTF-SD's superior performance and stability.
Replacing rich text critiques with correctness-only feedback significantly degrades RLTF-SD's performance, confirming the importance of detailed natural language feedback.
RLTF-FM successfully enables iterative self-refinement at inference time through self-generated critiques, leading to further performance gains on tasks like Knights and Knaves and MATH500.
solution: The paper formalizes Reinforcement Learning from Text Feedback (RLTF) as a paradigm to leverage natural language critiques, which are richer than scalar rewards yet cheaper than full demonstrations.
RLTF-SD (Self Distillation) distills the policy's feedback-conditioned competence (e.g., improved second-turn outputs) into the initial policy to enhance single-turn performance.
RLTF-FM (Feedback Modeling) trains the LLM to predict the textual feedback itself via a cross-entropy objective, which serves as a representation preconditioner and enables test-time self-critique.
Developed robust distillation techniques for RLTF-SD, including an effective first-turn baseline and an AWR-style objective that prioritizes lower variance over unbiased importance weighting.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Carnegie Mellon University, Inria, and Aurora Innovation developed Reinforcement Learning from Text Feedback (RLTF), using Self Distillation and Feedback Modeling to enable large language models to internalize rich natural language critiques. This approach yields substantial improvements in single-turn test-time performance across diverse tasks, moving beyond the limitations of sparse scalar rewards.

title: Expanding the Capabilities of Reinforcement Learning via Text Feedback

abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) is appealing for large language model (LLM) post-training because it evaluates teacher feedback on student-generated rollouts rather than fixed teacher traces. In long-horizon settings, however, the common sampled-token variant is fragile: it reduces distribution matching to a one-token signal and becomes increasingly unreliable as rollouts drift away from prefixes the teacher commonly visits. We revisit OPD from the estimator and implementation sides. Theoretically, token-level OPD is biased relative to sequence-level reverse-KL, but it has a much tighter worst-case variance bound; our toy study shows the same tradeoff empirically, with stronger future-reward coupling producing higher gradient variance and less stable learning. Empirically, we identify three failure modes of sampled-token OPD: an imbalanced one-token signal, unreliable teacher guidance on student-generated prefixes, and distortions caused by tokenizer or special-token mismatch. We address these issues with teacher top-K local support matching, implemented as truncated reverse-KL with top-p rollout sampling and special-token masking. Across single-task math reasoning and multi-task agentic-plus-math training, this objective yields more stable optimization and better downstream performance than sampled-token OPD.

keyInsights: Token-level OPD, while biased, exhibits a significantly lower worst-case variance ($O(T^2)$) compared to the unbiased sequence-level OPD ($O(T^4)$), which helps explain its practical stability in long-horizon tasks.
Empirical sampled-token OPD suffers from an imbalanced reward signal, unreliable teacher feedback on student-generated (potentially OOD) prefixes, and sensitivity to tokenizer mismatches.

A distribution-level comparison over a local teacher-supported set, combined with practical stabilization techniques like renormalization and top-p sampling, leads to a more robust and stable on-policy distillation objective.
originalProblem: The sampled-token variant of on-policy distillation (OPD) for LLMs often produces a highly localized and imbalanced one-token signal, leading to optimization dominated by a few tokens, often with negative rewards.

Teacher guidance can become unreliable on student-generated prefixes that are out-of-distribution for the teacher, causing the student to follow undesirable trajectories like repetition loops despite locally plausible teacher probabilities.

Distortions arise from tokenizer or special-token mismatches between teacher and student models, where semantically correct student tokens receive spurious penalties due to different segmentation conventions.
results: The proposed method achieved higher average scores in single-task math reasoning (41.5 vs. 40.7 for masked sampled-token baseline) and multi-task training (41.7 math score with 95.3% ALFWorld success rate).
Optimization stability was enhanced, evidenced by smaller gradient norms, fewer instances of gradient clipping, and maintained policy entropy compared to the baseline sampled-token OPD.

The new objective showed reduced sensitivity to tokenizer mismatch issues, with special-token masking having only a modest impact on its performance (41.0 without mask vs. 41.5 with mask), unlike the substantial gains observed for the sampled-token baseline with masking.
solution: Introduced "teacher top-K local support matching," an objective that replaces the single-token comparison with a distribution-level comparison over a teacher-selected support set for each prefix.
Implemented support-set renormalization to ensure comparability of probability masses within the truncated distributions, which is crucial for training stability.

Utilized top-p rollout sampling to guide student trajectory generation, ensuring rollouts stay within regions where teacher feedback is more reliable, and masked problematic special tokens to prevent spurious penalties.
paper_summary.summary: A study analyzing on-policy distillation (OPD) for large language models uncovered empirical issues with the sampled-token approach, such as imbalanced feedback and unreliable teacher signals. It introduces a "teacher top-K local support matching" objective, which provides more stable optimization and enhances performance in long-horizon tasks like math reasoning and multi-task agentic training.

title: Revisiting On-Policy Distillation: Empirical Failure Modes and Simple Fixes

abstract: Training reasoning language models (LMs) with reinforcement learning (RL) for one-hot correctness inherently relies on the LM being able to explore and solve its task with some chance at initialization. Furthermore, a key use case of reasoning LMs is to act as teachers for distilling new students and cold-starting future RL iterations rather than being deployed themselves. From these considerations, we introduce a new framework that avoids RL's exploration challenge by training a new class of Reinforcement-Learned Teachers (RLTs) focused on yielding the most effective downstream distillation. RLTs are prompted with both the question and solution to each problem, and tasked to simply "connect-the-dots" with detailed explanations tailored for their students. We train RLTs with dense rewards obtained by feeding each explanation to the student and testing its understanding of the problem's solution. In practice, the raw outputs of a 7B RLT provide higher final performance on competition and graduate-level tasks than existing distillation and cold-starting pipelines that collect and postprocess the reasoning traces of orders of magnitude larger LMs. Furthermore, RLTs maintain their effectiveness when training larger students and when applied zero-shot to out-of-distribution tasks, unlocking new levels of efficiency and re-usability for the RL reasoning framework. Code available at: this https URL

keyInsights: Reframing the reinforcement learning objective to focus on explanation generation for known solutions effectively circumvents the severe exploration challenges faced by traditional RL with sparse correctness rewards.

Aligning the teacher's training objective directly with its downstream distillation role, by optimizing for explanation quality, yields superior and more efficient knowledge transfer to student models.
Even relatively small language models possess latent 'teaching skills' that can be effectively elicited and refined through purpose-aligned dense reward functions.
originalProblem: Traditional reinforcement learning for reasoning Language Models (LMs) struggles with sparse, one-hot correctness rewards, leading to exploration challenges, instability, and high computational costs, often only benefiting very large models.

Current supervised distillation methods for LMs face an objective mismatch where teachers optimized for problem-solving do not optimally generate instructive explanations for students, necessitating extensive heuristic postprocessing.
Existing distillation pipelines are inefficient and rely on prohibitively large LMs or costly manual/LLM-based postprocessing for data refinement.
results: Raw explanations from a 7B RLT consistently led to higher student performance on challenging reasoning tasks (AIME, MATH, GPQA) compared to distillation from larger LMs (e.g., DeepSeek R1) with extensive postprocessing.

RLT-generated traces proved more effective for cold-starting traditional reinforcement learning and exhibited strong zero-shot transferability, enabling successful distillation for out-of-domain tasks.

The framework achieved substantial computational efficiency, with training a 7B RLT and generating its dataset requiring only a fraction of the GPU hours compared to existing large-scale reasoning LM pipelines.
solution: Redefine the teacher's role by training Reinforcement-Learned Teachers (RLTs) to generate step-by-step explanations for given question-solution pairs, rather than solving problems independently.

Introduce a dense, two-part reward function (r_SS for student understanding and r_KL for explanation interpretability) to provide continuous feedback on the quality and utility of the generated explanations for student learning.
Employ a computationally efficient training paradigm using the GRPO algorithm with a small 7B base model, requiring minimal pre-training and a short reinforcement learning phase.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Sakana AI developed Reinforcement-Learned Teachers (RLTs), a framework that trains specialized language models to generate high-quality, step-by-step explanations for known solutions instead of solving problems from scratch. This approach produces superior distillation datasets, enabling smaller student models to outperform those trained with existing pipelines based on much larger LMs, and achieves significant computational efficiency with an RLT training costing 280.4 GPU-hours.

title: Reinforcement Learning Teachers of Test Time Scaling

abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD), which aligns the student with the teacher's logit distribution on student-generated trajectories, has demonstrated strong empirical gains in improving student performance and often outperforms off-policy distillation and reinforcement learning (RL) paradigms. In this work, we first theoretically show that OPD is a special case of dense KL-constrained RL where the reward function and the KL regularization are always weighted equally and the reference model can by any model. Then, we propose the Generalized On-Policy Distillation (G-OPD) framework, which extends the standard OPD objective by introducing a flexible reference model and a reward scaling factor that controls the relative weight of the reward term against the KL regularization. Through comprehensive experiments on math reasoning and code generation tasks, we derive two novel insights: (1) Setting the reward scaling factor to be greater than 1 (i.e., reward extrapolation), which we term ExOPD, consistently improves over standard OPD across a range of teacher-student size pairings. In particular, in the setting where we merge the knowledge from different domain experts, obtained by applying domain-specific RL to the same student model, back into the original student, ExOPD enables the student to even surpass the teacher's performance boundary and outperform the domain teachers. (2) Building on ExOPD, we further find that in the strong-to-weak distillation setting (i.e., distilling a smaller student from a larger teacher), performing reward correction by choosing the reference model as the teacher's base model before RL yields a more accurate reward signal and further improves distillation performance. However, this choice assumes access to the teacher's pre-RL variant and incurs more computational overhead. We hope our work offers new insights for future research on OPD.

keyInsights: Standard On-Policy Distillation is a specific instance of dense KL-constrained Reinforcement Learning where the implicit reward and KL regularization are equally weighted.
Tuning the reward scaling factor (λ) in G-OPD allows for controlled behavior, with λ < 1 enabling reward interpolation and λ > 1 (ExOPD) facilitating reward extrapolation beyond teacher capabilities.

Using the teacher's pre-RL base model as the reference (reward correction) provides a more accurate reward signal for strong-to-weak distillation, improving student learning despite higher computational costs.
originalProblem: Existing On-Policy Distillation (OPD) methods lacked a robust theoretical foundation, hindering full understanding and further generalization.
Standard OPD had fixed reward-KL regularization weighting, limiting flexibility in how students learn from teachers.
Distilling knowledge from multiple specialized teachers into a single student or from strong teachers to weak students often results in student performance bounded by the teacher's capabilities.
results: ExOPD (λ=1.25) consistently outperformed standard OPD and domain teachers, achieving an average relative performance gain of up to 10.9% on math reasoning (e.g., AIME24) and 2.1% on code generation (e.g., HumanEval+).
In multi-teacher distillation, ExOPD was the only method that yielded a single student model surpassing the performance of all individual domain-specific teachers.

ExOPD effectively distilled knowledge from a 30B teacher to 1.7B and 4B students, and 'reward correction' (using the teacher's pre-RL base model as reference) further boosted performance, enhancing the robustness of strong-to-weak distillation.
solution: The paper re-derived the standard OPD objective, proving its equivalence to a dense KL-constrained RL problem with a fixed 1:1 reward-KL weighting and a flexible reference model (π_ref).
A Generalized On-Policy Distillation (G-OPD) framework was proposed, introducing a tunable reward scaling factor (λ) and explicitly allowing a flexible choice of π_ref.

The G-OPD framework was applied to multi-teacher distillation using reward extrapolation (ExOPD, λ > 1) and to strong-to-weak distillation, exploring the impact of a 'reward correction' mechanism (setting π_ref to the teacher's pre-RL base model).
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Renmin University of China and Tencent established a theoretical link between On-Policy Distillation (OPD) and dense KL-constrained Reinforcement Learning, proposing a Generalized On-Policy Distillation (G-OPD) framework. This framework, through reward extrapolation (ExOPD), enabled student models to surpass teacher model performance by up to 10.9% on math reasoning benchmarks and 2.1% on code generation, and effectively merged multi-teacher capabilities.

title: Learning beyond Teacher: Generalized On-Policy Distillation with Reward Extrapolation

abstract: Knowledge distillation has become a primary mechanism for transferring reasoning and domain expertise from frontier Large Language Models (LLMs) to smaller, deployable students. However, the dominant paradigm remains \textit{off-policy}: students train on static teacher-generated data and never encounter their own errors during learning. This train--test mismatch, an instance of \textit{exposure bias}, causes prediction errors to compound autoregressively at inference time. On-Policy Distillation (OPD) addresses this by letting the student generate its own trajectories and receive teacher feedback on these self-generated outputs, grounding distillation in the theory of interactive imitation learning. Despite rapid growth spanning divergence minimization, reward-guided learning, and self-play, the OPD literature remains fragmented with no unified treatment. This survey provides the first comprehensive overview of OPD for LLMs. We introduce a unified $f$-divergence framework over on-policy samples and organize the landscape along three orthogonal dimensions: \emph{feedback signal} (logit-based, outcome-based, or self-play), \emph{teacher access} (white-box, black-box, or teacher-free), and \emph{loss granularity} (token-level, sequence-level, or hybrid). We systematically analyze representative methods, examine industrial deployments, and identify open problems including distillation scaling laws, uncertainty-aware feedback, and agent-level distillation.

keyInsights: On-policy learning fundamentally addresses exposure bias, reducing error accumulation in autoregressive generation from quadratic (O(T^2)) to linear (O(T)) by allowing students to learn from their own generated trajectories.
The specific choice of f-divergence (e.g., Forward KL for mode-covering vs. Reverse KL for mode-seeking) is crucial, influencing the student's output diversity and coherence.

The distinction between knowledge distillation and reinforcement learning is dissolving, with increasing integration into hybrid frameworks that leverage stable imitation from KD and exploration from RL.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) demand extensive computational resources, creating barriers to widespread deployment.
Off-policy knowledge distillation methods face "exposure bias," where student models accumulate quadratic errors in autoregressive generation due to train-test mismatches.
The rapidly growing field of On-Policy Distillation (OPD) for LLMs lacked a comprehensive, unified framework to organize diverse methods and identify key challenges.
results: On-Policy Distillation (OPD) consistently improves performance by mitigating exposure bias, transforming error accumulation from quadratic to linear in sequence length, particularly for multi-step reasoning tasks.

The survey unifies diverse OPD methods under a generalized f-divergence framework, highlighting that adaptive divergence approaches often outperform fixed choices by balancing diversity and coherence.

A novel three-dimensional taxonomy provides a structured classification of OPD methods based on feedback signal, teacher access, and loss granularity, clarifying the design space and identifying open research problems.
solution: Formalizes autoregressive language generation within a Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework to precisely define and quantify exposure bias.
Proposes a unified theoretical framework for On-Policy Distillation (OPD) using generalized f-divergence to characterize various objective functions.
Introduces a novel three-dimensional taxonomy to categorize OPD methods based on feedback signal, teacher access, and loss granularity.
paper_summary.summary: A comprehensive survey outlines On-Policy Distillation (OPD) for Large Language Models (LLMs), introducing a unified theoretical framework based on f-divergences and a three-dimensional taxonomy for existing methods. The work details how OPD mitigates exposure bias, reducing error accumulation from quadratic to linear, and explores various methodologies and future research directions for efficient and capable LLM distillation.

title: A Survey of On-Policy Distillation for Large Language Models

abstract:
Post-training of language models (LMs) increasingly relies on the following
two stages: (i) knowledge distillation, where the LM is trained to imitate a
larger teacher LM, and (ii) reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF),
where the LM is aligned by optimizing a reward model. In the second RLHF stage,
a well-known challenge is reward hacking, where the LM over-optimizes the
reward model. Such phenomenon is in line with Goodhart's law and can lead to
degraded performance on the true objective. In this paper, we investigate
whether a similar phenomenon, that we call teacher hacking, can occur during
knowledge distillation. This could arise because the teacher LM is itself an
imperfect approximation of the true distribution. To study this, we propose a
controlled experimental setup involving: (i) an oracle LM representing the
ground-truth distribution, (ii) a teacher LM distilled from the oracle, and
(iii) a student LM distilled from the teacher. Our experiments reveal the
following insights. When using a fixed offline dataset for distillation,
teacher hacking occurs; moreover, we can detect it by observing when the
optimization process deviates from polynomial convergence laws. In contrast,
employing online data generation techniques effectively mitigates teacher
hacking. More precisely, we identify data diversity as the key factor in
preventing hacking. Overall, our findings provide a deeper understanding of the
benefits and limitations of distillation for building robust and efficient LMs.

keyInsights: Teacher hacking occurs when a student model over-optimizes to a static, imperfect teacher, leading to a U-shaped performance curve where true performance degrades despite improved teacher alignment.
Dynamic online data generation during distillation effectively mitigates teacher hacking by continuously providing fresh data, preventing over-optimization to fixed imperfections.
Increasing data diversity, either by using more unique prompts or generating multiple completions per prompt in offline settings, also reduces the impact of teacher hacking.
originalProblem: Knowledge distillation (KD) for language models often assumes the teacher model perfectly represents the true data distribution or that faithful imitation is always the optimal goal.
The teacher model itself is an imperfect approximation of the true distribution or human intent, potentially containing noise or biases from its own training.
While 'reward hacking' is a known issue in RLHF, a similar over-optimization phenomenon in the earlier KD stage, where students exploit teacher imperfections, was previously under-investigated.
results: Empirically confirmed the existence of teacher hacking, observed as a U-shaped performance curve (true performance vs. teacher alignment) when training for extended epochs on fixed offline teacher-generated data.
Demonstrated that online data generation, whether from the teacher or student, consistently prevents teacher hacking, leading to continuous improvement in both proxy and golden metrics.
Showed that even a small proportion (10-50%) of online-generated data mixed with offline data can substantially reduce or eliminate teacher hacking, offering a practical mitigation strategy.
solution: Introduced a controlled experimental setup featuring an 'oracle model' to represent the true ground-truth distribution, allowing for direct measurement of a student model's actual performance.
Designed a two-stage training pipeline: initial supervised fine-tuning (SFT) from the oracle for model checkpoints, followed by knowledge distillation (KD) from the teacher to the student.
Investigated various data generation strategies for distillation, including fixed offline data and dynamic online data generated by the teacher or student during training.
paper_summary.summary: The paper empirically identifies and characterizes 'teacher hacking' in language model distillation, a phenomenon where student models over-optimize to imperfections in a fixed teacher, causing a decline in true performance. It demonstrates that employing online data generation or increasing data diversity effectively mitigates this issue, leading to better alignment to the ground truth.

title: On Teacher Hacking in Language Model Distillation

abstract:
Distilling large language models (LLMs) typically involves transferring the
teacher model's responses through supervised fine-tuning (SFT). However, this
approach neglects the potential to distill both data (output content) and
reward signals (quality evaluations). Extracting reliable reward signals
directly from teacher models is challenging, as LLMs are optimized for
generation rather than evaluation, often resulting in biased or inconsistent
assessments. To address this limitation, we propose a novel distillation
pipeline that transfers both responses and rewards. Our method generates
pseudo-rewards through a self-supervised mechanism that leverages the inherent
structure of both teacher and student responses, enabling reward learning
without explicit external evaluation. The reward model subsequently guides
reinforcement learning (RL), allowing iterative refinement of the student model
after an SFT warm-up phase. Experiments on GSM8K and MMLU-PRO demonstrate that
our method consistently outperforms traditional SFT-based approaches, enabling
student models to surpass the performance of their teachers. This work
highlights the potential for scalable, efficient distillation through
structured self-supervised reward learning, reducing dependence on external
reward supervision.

keyInsights: Distilling both knowledge and reward signals leads to more effective transfer than traditional methods
Self-supervised reward learning can reduce dependence on human feedback
Careful handling of distribution shift between teacher and student outputs is crucial for successful distillation
originalProblem: Traditional knowledge distillation methods only transfer model outputs, not reward signals, limiting their effectiveness
Training smaller language models to match larger ones' performance typically requires extensive external reward supervision
Distribution shift between teacher and student outputs poses challenges for effective knowledge transfer
results: 3-5% improvement over traditional methods on GSM8K benchmark
Up to 9% improvement on MMLU-PRO in some configurations
Student models occasionally outperforming their teachers, demonstrating the effectiveness of combined data and reward distillation
Consistent performance improvements across different model sizes and tasks
solution: Three-phase pipeline combining data distillation, reward model learning, and reinforcement learning optimization
Self-supervised reward design incorporating classification and preference learning
Majority voting mechanism for self-evaluation and high-quality response filtering
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Microsoft and leading universities introduce a groundbreaking knowledge distillation framework that transfers both model outputs and reward signals from large language models to smaller ones, achieving up to 9% improvement on key benchmarks while enabling student models to occasionally outperform their teachers through novel self-supervised reward learning techniques.

title: Distill Not Only Data but Also Rewards: Can Smaller Language Models  Surpass Larger Ones?

abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a standard paradigm for post-training large language models. While Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is widely adopted, its coarse credit assignment uniformly penalizes failed rollouts, lacking the token-level focus needed to efficiently address specific deviations. Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO) addresses this by providing denser, more targeted logit-level supervision that facilitates rapid early improvement, yet it frequently collapses during prolonged training. We trace this late-stage instability to two intrinsic flaws: self-distillation on already-correct samples introduces optimization ambiguity, and the self-teacher's signal reliability progressively degrades. To resolve these issues, we propose Sample-Routed Policy Optimization (SRPO), a unified on-policy framework that routes correct samples to GRPO's reward-aligned reinforcement and failed samples to SDPO's targeted logit-level correction. SRPO further incorporates an entropy-aware dynamic weighting mechanism to suppress high-entropy, unreliable distillation targets while emphasizing confident ones. Evaluated across five benchmarks and two model scales, SRPO achieves both the rapid early improvement of SDPO and the long-horizon stability of GRPO. It consistently surpasses the peak performance of both baselines, raising the five-benchmark average on Qwen3-8B by 3.4% over GRPO and 6.3% over SDPO, while simultaneously yielding moderate response lengths and lowering per-step compute cost by up to 17.2%.

keyInsights: GRPO provides stable, reward-aligned updates suitable for successful behaviors, while SDPO offers dense, logit-level corrections effective for repairing specific errors in failed trajectories.
SDPO's late-stage instability arises from optimization ambiguity when distilling correct samples and the decreasing reliability (increasing entropy) of the self-teacher's signal over time.

Dynamically routing samples to the most appropriate optimization signal (GRPO for correct, SDPO for incorrect with reliable teacher) and weighting distillation targets by confidence can combine the benefits of both approaches.
originalProblem: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) uses coarse, sequence-level credit assignment, leading to inefficient updates for failed rollouts and slower convergence.

Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO) offers fast early convergence but experiences instability and performance collapse during prolonged training due to ambiguous signals on correct samples and degrading teacher quality.
Post-training methods for LLMs often struggle to balance the stability of reward-aligned policy gradient methods with the fine-grained guidance of self-distillation.
results: SRPO achieved an average accuracy of 77.4% on Qwen3-8B across five benchmarks, surpassing GRPO by 3.4 percentage points and SDPO by 6.3 percentage points.
The method demonstrated rapid early performance gains similar to SDPO, combined with long-horizon stability and higher peak accuracy, avoiding the performance collapse seen in SDPO.
SRPO reduced per-step compute time by 17.2% compared to GRPO and 9.4% compared to SDPO after 10 hours of training, while also yielding moderate and efficient response lengths.
solution: Introduces Sample-Routed Policy Optimization (SRPO), an on-policy framework that unifies GRPO and SDPO by adaptively applying them.
Employs a sample routing mechanism that directs correct samples to a GRPO-based objective and incorrect samples with available teacher information to an SDPO-based objective.
Integrates an entropy-aware dynamic weighting mechanism within the self-distillation branch to reduce reliance on unreliable (high-entropy) teacher signals, improving late-stage stability.
paper_summary.summary: Sample-Routed Policy Optimization (SRPO) unifies Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO) for large language model post-training. This framework uses adaptive sample routing and entropy-aware dynamic weighting to improve early training efficiency, enhance long-horizon stability, and achieve higher peak performance on various benchmarks.

title: Unifying Group-Relative and Self-Distillation Policy Optimization via Sample Routing

abstract: Training-time privileged information (PI) can enable language models to succeed on tasks they would otherwise fail, making it a powerful tool for reinforcement learning in hard, long-horizon settings. However, transferring capabilities learned with PI to policies that must act without it at inference time remains a fundamental challenge. We study this problem in the context of distilling frontier models for multi-turn agentic environments, which typically hide their internal reasoning and expose only action trajectories. This breaks standard distillation pipelines, since successful behavior is observable, but the reasoning process is not. For this, we introduce {\pi}-Distill, a joint teacher-student objective that trains a PI-conditioned teacher and an unconditioned student simultaneously using the same model. Additionally, we also introduce On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), an alternative approach that trains using Reinforcement Learning (RL) with a reverse KL-penalty between the student and the PI-conditioned teacher. We show that both of these algorithms effectively distill frontier agents using action-only PI. Specifically, we find that {\pi}-Distill and, in some cases, OPSD, outperform industry standard practices (Supervised finetuning followed by RL) that assume access to full Chain-of-Thought supervision across multiple agentic benchmarks, models, and forms of PI. We complement our results with extensive analysis that characterizes the factors enabling effective learning with PI, focusing primarily on {\pi}-Distill and characterizing when OPSD is competitive.

keyInsights: Effective knowledge transfer from black-box frontier models is achievable using only action-based privileged information, circumventing the need for explicit Chain-of-Thought traces.
A shared-parameter model jointly optimizing PI-conditioned teacher and unconditioned student policies offers a more efficient and unified training process for distillation.

The effectiveness of PI learning is influenced by factors such as initial KL divergence between policies, the balance between teacher and student objectives, and the information density of the privileged information.
originalProblem: Large language models often struggle with complex, long-horizon reinforcement learning tasks due to sparse rewards and exploration difficulties.
Distilling knowledge from powerful, opaque frontier models is challenging when their internal reasoning processes (Chain-of-Thought) are inaccessible.
Traditional distillation methods can be inefficient, requiring sequential training of separate teacher and student models with extensive hyperparameter tuning.
results: π-Distill variants consistently outperformed the 'SFT with CoT + RL' baseline, showing up to 11.8% improvement on Travel Planner and 2.08% to 6.00% on τ-Bench.
The proposed methods demonstrated strong out-of-domain generalization capabilities on the GEM search-tool benchmark suite, preventing performance degradation observed in standard RL.
Training efficiency was improved, with π-Distill requiring only a single training phase, and larger models (Qwen3-8B) exhibited enhanced benefits from the on-policy distillation approaches.
solution: Introduced π-Distill and On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) algorithms that use a single model with shared parameters to act as both a PI-conditioned teacher and an unconditioned student.
π-Distill optimizes a joint objective, training the teacher to maximize reward with PI while regularizing it towards the student, and the student learns from teacher-generated trajectories.

OPSD, an on-policy alternative, updates the student to maximize environment reward while regularizing it to match the PI-conditioned teacher policy, using a reverse KL as a dense per-token reward signal.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed π-Distill and On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) algorithms to transfer capabilities from black-box large language models to smaller models using only observed actions as privileged information, without requiring internal Chain-of-Thought traces. These methods achieved up to 11.8% higher performance on Travel Planner and 2.08% to 6.00% on τ-Bench compared to baselines that rely on explicit CoT.

title: Privileged Information Distillation for Language Models

abstract: Long context reasoning in large language models (LLMs) has demonstrated enhancement of their cognitive capabilities via chain-of-thought (CoT) inference. Training such models is usually done via reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) in reasoning based problems, like math and programming. However, RLVR is limited by several bottlenecks, such as, lack of dense reward, and inadequate sample efficiency. As a result, it requires significant compute resources in post-training phase. To overcome these limitations, in this work, we propose \textbf{Semantic Soft Bootstrapping (SSB)}, a self-distillation technique, in which the same base language model plays the role of both teacher and student, but receives different semantic contexts about the correctness of its outcome at training time. The model is first prompted with a math problem and several rollouts are generated. From them, the correct and most common incorrect response are filtered, and then provided to the model in context to produce a more robust, step-by-step explanation with a verified final answer. This pipeline automatically curates a paired teacher-student training set from raw problem-answer data, without any human intervention. This generation process also produces a sequence of logits, which is what the student model tries to match in the training phase just from the bare question alone. In our experiment, Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct on GSM8K dataset via parameter-efficient fine-tuning. We then tested its accuracy on MATH500, and AIME2024 benchmarks. Our experiments show a jump of 10.6%, and 10% improvements in accuracy, respectively, over group relative policy optimization (GRPO), which is a commonly used RLVR algorithm. Our code is available at this https URL, and the model, curated dataset is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Significant improvements in LLM reasoning capabilities can be achieved through self-supervision and knowledge distillation, bypassing the complexities and limitations of reinforcement learning.
Enhanced reasoning does not necessitate longer chains-of-thought; SSB achieves superior performance without increasing the average completion length.
Leveraging the LLM's in-context learning to contrast correct and incorrect examples provides a dense, per-token learning signal, offering a more robust alternative to sparse outcome-based rewards.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) in LLMs suffers from sparse and coarse rewards, leading to inefficient learning and potential reward hacking.
RLVR-based post-training for LLMs is computationally expensive and sample inefficient, requiring vast numbers of rollouts and significant resources.
Existing RLVR methods may not fundamentally improve core reasoning abilities and can result in undesirably long or verbose chains-of-thought.
results: SSB training achieved a 55.4% pass@1 accuracy on the MATH500 benchmark, a 10.6% improvement over the 44.8% accuracy of the GRPO baseline.
On the AIME2024 benchmark, SSB demonstrated a 13.33% pass@1 accuracy, a 10% increase compared to GRPO's 3.33% performance.

The method exhibited superior sample efficiency, outperforming GRPO with a curated dataset of only 256 samples versus GRPO's 2000 samples, and maintained stable training dynamics without increased completion length.
solution: The proposed Semantic Soft Bootstrapping (SSB) framework is an RL-free self-distillation method where the same base LLM functions as both teacher and student.

A "teacher pipeline" generates multiple candidate solutions, identifies correct and incorrect ones, and then prompts the LLM to synthesize a robust, didactic explanation by contrasting these examples.

The "student training" phase fine-tunes the base LLM using LoRA adapters to match the precomputed logit-level soft distributions of the teacher's refined solutions, leveraging knowledge distillation without explicit policy gradients.
paper_summary.summary: Semantic Soft Bootstrapping (SSB), an RL-free self-distillation framework developed at the University of Maryland, enhances large language model reasoning by having the model act as both teacher and student. It boosted pass@1 accuracy on the MATH500 benchmark by 10.6% and on AIME2024 by 10% over a GRPO baseline, while utilizing a smaller dataset and maintaining concise response lengths.

title: Semantic Soft Bootstrapping: Long Context Reasoning in LLMs without Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has recently emerged as a powerful paradigm for facilitating the self-improvement of large language models (LLMs), particularly in the domain of complex reasoning tasks. However, prevailing on-policy RL methods often contend with significant training instability and inefficiency. This is primarily due to a capacity-difficulty mismatch, where the complexity of training data frequently outpaces the model's current capabilities, leading to critically sparse reward signals and stalled learning progress. This challenge is particularly acute for smaller, more resource-efficient LLMs. To overcome this, we introduce the Guided Hybrid Policy Optimization (GHPO), a novel difficulty-aware reinforcement learning framework. GHPO dynamically calibrates task difficulty by employing adaptive prompt refinement to provide targeted guidance. This unique approach adaptively balances direct imitation learning for problems currently beyond the model's reach with exploration-based reinforcement learning for more manageable tasks, effectively creating a smooth and optimized learning curriculum. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GHPO achieves an average performance gain of approximately 5% across six challenging mathematics benchmarks, consistently outperforming strong on-policy reinforcement learning and curriculum learning baselines. Further analysis confirms that our framework significantly enhances both training stability and final reasoning performance, thus offering a scalable and efficient solution for developing powerful and robust reasoning models.

keyInsights: Adaptive guidance, by providing partial ground-truth hints based on detected difficulty, effectively addresses reward sparsity in LLM reinforcement learning.
Dynamically assessing problem difficulty on-the-fly and adapting guidance levels leads to more stable and efficient training than static curriculum or data-discarding methods.
Integrating targeted imitation learning for difficult tasks with exploration-based reinforcement learning for manageable tasks creates a robust and scalable learning curriculum.
originalProblem: On-policy Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) for LLMs suffers from reward sparsity due to a mismatch between training data difficulty and the model's current capabilities, leading to zero rewards and stalled learning.
Existing methods like curriculum learning often require manual, heuristic-based data partitioning, which is not scalable or adaptively responsive to evolving model performance.
Other approaches, such as dynamic sampling, discard valuable training data, leading to inefficiency, or rely on auxiliary resource-intensive LLMs, increasing training costs.
results: GHPO improved the average accuracy of Qwen2.5-7B by 4.4% over standard GRPO (from 0.398 to 0.442) on medium-difficulty math tasks (Math3to5 dataset) across six benchmarks.

On challenging tasks (NuminaMath-S dataset), GHPO consistently outperformed GRPO and curriculum learning baselines, achieving a substantial accuracy improvement on highly challenging AIME2024 problems (0.163 vs. GRPO's 0.122).

Training dynamics showed GHPO consistently maintained smaller gradient magnitudes compared to GRPO, indicating a smoother and more stable optimization process while achieving higher accuracy rewards and enabling the generation of longer responses.
solution: GHPO introduces a two-module framework: Automated Difficulty Detection identifies 'difficult' queries where all sampled responses yield zero rewards.

Adaptive Prompt Refinement with Multi-Stage Guidance appends a specific proportion of the ground-truth solution (hint) to difficult queries, progressively increasing the hint ratio (e.g., 0.25 to 0.75) across stages if problems remain unsolved.

The framework optimizes the policy using a modified Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) objective, conditioning policy updates on these refined, hint-augmented queries, ensuring a non-zero learning signal for challenging problems.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Huawei Research and City University of Hong Kong developed Guided Hybrid Policy Optimization (GHPO), a framework for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) that enhances LLM stability and efficiency by adaptively providing partial ground-truth hints for difficult problems. GHPO improved the average accuracy of a Qwen2.5-7B model by 4.4% on medium-difficulty math tasks and consistently outperformed baselines on challenging math benchmarks while maintaining more stable training dynamics.

title: GHPO: Adaptive Guidance for Stable and Efficient LLM Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is increasingly used to align large language models (LLMs). Off-policy methods offer greater implementation simplicity and data efficiency than on-policy techniques, but often result in suboptimal performance. In this work, we study the intermediate range of algorithms between off-policy RL and supervised fine-tuning by analyzing a simple off-policy REINFORCE algorithm, where the advantage is defined as $A=r-V$, with $r$ a reward and $V$ some tunable baseline. Intuitively, lowering $V$ emphasizes high-reward samples, while raising it penalizes low-reward ones more heavily. We first provide a theoretical analysis of this off-policy REINFORCE algorithm, showing that when the baseline $V$ lower-bounds the expected reward, the algorithm enjoys a policy improvement guarantee. Our analysis reveals that while on-policy updates can safely leverage both positive and negative signals, off-policy updates benefit from focusing more on positive rewards than on negative ones. We validate our findings experimentally in a controlled stochastic bandit setting and through fine-tuning state-of-the-art LLMs on reasoning tasks.

keyInsights: The baseline 'V' in off-policy REINFORCE fundamentally alters the learning objective and asymptotic policy, going beyond mere variance reduction.
A critical 'phase transition' occurs when 'V' equals or exceeds the expected reward of the behavior policy ($V_μ$), causing a drastic and detrimental loss of policy diversity and support.
Setting 'V' strictly below $V_μ$ is essential to guarantee policy improvement, maintain policy diversity, and ensure stable, non-collapsing learning dynamics in off-policy settings.
originalProblem: On-policy reinforcement learning (RL) methods, common for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs), are highly sample-inefficient and computationally expensive.
Existing off-policy RL techniques for LLMs, while data-efficient, often struggle with stability and performance degradation due to distribution shift and high variance from importance sampling.
A demand exists for simple, yet robust, off-policy RL algorithms for LLMs that prevent common training instabilities and collapses.
results:
Theoretical analysis and multi-armed bandit experiments confirmed that setting $V 
ot< V_μ$ leads to policies with drastically reduced support and suboptimal convergence.
LLM fine-tuning experiments (Llama 8B, Llama 3B, Qwen 3B) on reasoning tasks showed that AsymRE with a conservative baseline ($\delta V < 0$) achieved stable training and higher test accuracies.

Conversely, setting the baseline such that $V 
ot< V_μ$ (i.e., $\delta V \ge 0$) consistently resulted in catastrophic training collapses, rapid drops in policy entropy, and significantly reduced performance.
solution: Introduces Asymmetric REINFORCE (AsymRE), a simple off-policy policy gradient algorithm that avoids complex importance sampling corrections.
Analyzes the crucial role of the tunable baseline 'V' within the advantage term `(r(y) − V)` to balance positive and negative reward signals during off-policy updates.
Proposes that prioritizing positive reward signals by setting 'V' to be strictly less than the behavior policy's expected reward can lead to more stable and effective off-policy learning.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from FAIR at Meta and NYU developed Asymmetric REINFORCE (AsymRE), a simple off-policy reinforcement learning algorithm that stabilizes large language model fine-tuning. The method demonstrates that carefully selecting the baseline 'V' to be strictly less than the behavior policy's expected reward prevents catastrophic training collapses and improves accuracy in complex reasoning tasks.

title: Asymmetric REINFORCE for off-Policy Reinforcement Learning: Balancing positive and negative rewards

abstract:
Recent advances in large language model (LLM) post-training have leveraged
two distinct paradigms to enhance reasoning capabilities: reinforcement
learning (RL) and knowledge distillation (KD). While RL enables the emergence
of complex reasoning behaviors, it often suffers from low sample efficiency
when the initial policy struggles to explore high-reward trajectories.
Conversely, KD improves learning efficiency via mimicking the teacher model but
tends to generalize poorly to out-of-domain scenarios. In this work, we present
\textbf{KDRL}, a \textit{unified post-training framework} that jointly
optimizes a reasoning model through teacher supervision (KD) and
self-exploration (RL). Specifically, KDRL leverages policy gradient
optimization to simultaneously minimize the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence
(RKL) between the student and teacher distributions while maximizing the
expected rule-based rewards. We first formulate a unified objective that
integrates GRPO and KD, and systematically explore how different KL
approximations, KL coefficients, and reward-guided KD strategies affect the
overall post-training dynamics and performance. Empirical results on multiple
reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that KDRL outperforms GRPO and various KD
baselines while achieving a favorable balance between performance and reasoning
token efficiency. These findings indicate that integrating KD and RL serves as
an effective and efficient strategy to train reasoning LLMs.

keyInsights: Joint optimization of RL and KD objectives within a unified framework is superior to sequential application or reward shaping, leading to more stable training and better performance.
The k2 estimator for reverse KL divergence approximation demonstrated superior stability and performance compared to the k3 estimator, despite being a biased estimator for the RKL itself.
A linearly annealed schedule for the KL coefficient (β) effectively balances early teacher guidance with later reward-driven exploration, yielding optimal results.
Reward-guided masking, particularly at the response level, significantly improves training and inference efficiency by focusing distillation on incorrect trajectories and reducing response length.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning (RL) for LLM post-training suffers from low sample efficiency, requiring extensive exploration and high computational costs for complex reasoning tasks.
Knowledge Distillation (KD) approaches offer high sample efficiency but face exposure bias and generalize poorly beyond the teacher's explicit knowledge.
Current methods typically apply RL and KD sequentially rather than jointly, leading to suboptimal utilization of their complementary strengths and inefficiencies in holistic optimization.
results: KDRL-Annealing consistently outperformed baselines, achieving a 4.7% improvement over SFT, 2.6% over GRPO, and 1.1% over KD-RKL on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
The joint loss formulation proved more stable and effective than reward shaping, which led to training instability and collapse.
Response-level reward-guided masking maintained accuracy while reducing response length by over 1,400 tokens (>10%), indicating enhanced efficiency.
KDRL demonstrated generalizability, showing consistent improvements when applied directly to pre-trained models without intermediate fine-tuning.
solution: KDRL unifies Knowledge Distillation (KD) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) into a single policy gradient scheme, optimizing both objectives jointly through a combined loss function.
It employs on-policy reverse Kullback-Leibler (RKL) divergence for knowledge distillation, sampling responses from the student's current policy to mitigate exposure bias.

The framework incorporates dynamic KL coefficient scheduling (e.g., linear annealing of β) and reward-guided KL masking to balance teacher guidance with reward-based optimization and prevent conflicting gradients.
paper_summary.summary: KDRL (Knowledge Distillation and Reinforcement Learning) introduces a unified post-training framework that integrates knowledge distillation and reinforcement learning to enhance reasoning capabilities in LLMs, achieving notable performance improvements and increased efficiency on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. This framework addresses the limitations of applying these methods separately by jointly optimizing both objectives.

title: KDRL: Post-Training Reasoning LLMs via Unified Knowledge Distillation  and Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Recent studies have uncovered the potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) in addressing complex sequential decision-making tasks through the provision of high-level instructions. However, LLM-based agents lack specialization in tackling specific target problems, particularly in real-time dynamic environments. Additionally, deploying an LLM-based agent in practical scenarios can be both costly and time-consuming. On the other hand, reinforcement learning (RL) approaches train agents that specialize in the target task but often suffer from low sampling efficiency and high exploration costs. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework that addresses these challenges by training a smaller, specialized student RL agent using instructions from an LLM-based teacher agent. By incorporating the guidance from the teacher agent, the student agent can distill the prior knowledge of the LLM into its own model. Consequently, the student agent can be trained with significantly less data. Moreover, through further training with environment feedback, the student agent surpasses the capabilities of its teacher for completing the target task. We conducted experiments on challenging MiniGrid and Habitat environments, specifically designed for embodied AI research, to evaluate the effectiveness of our framework. The results clearly demonstrate that our approach achieves superior performance compared to strong baseline methods. Our code is available at this https URL.
title: Large Language Model as a Policy Teacher for Training Reinforcement Learning Agents

abstract: Reinforcement learning in large language models (LLMs) often relies on scalar rewards, a practice that discards valuable textual rationale buried in the rollouts, forcing the model to explore \textit{de novo} with each attempt and hindering sample efficiency.
