While LLMs can uniquely learn from language feedback provided in-context, naively integrating on-line experiences into RL training presents a paradox: feedback from the same problem risks information leakage and memorization, while feedback from different problems often leads to behavior collapse due to irrelevant context. To resolve this tension, we propose \textbf{Language-And-Numerical Policy Optimization (LANPO)}, a framework that cleanly separates the roles of feedback: language guides exploration, while numerical rewards drive optimization. LANPO builds a dynamic experience pool from past trials and introduces two principles to ensure feedback is effective: \emph{Reward-Agnostic Reflection} for safe intra-sample self-correction and \emph{Relevant Abstraction} to distill generalizable lessons from inter-sample experiences. Across mathematical reasoning benchmarks, LANPO enables 7B and 14B models to significantly outperform strong baselines trained with GRPO in test accuracy. Our work provides a robust method for integrating historical experiences into the LLM RL loop, creating more effective and data-efficient learning agents.

keyInsights: Language and numerical feedback can synergistically enhance reinforcement learning when their roles are separated, with language guiding exploration and numerical rewards driving optimization.

Information leakage from intra-sample feedback and behavior collapse from inter-sample feedback can be systematically mitigated through specialized mechanisms: reward-agnostic self-reflection and relevance-filtered abstraction of experiences.
Distilling raw trajectories into abstract "principles and pitfalls" and explicitly prompting LLMs to engage with this knowledge accelerates policy search and improves generalization.
originalProblem: Existing LLM reinforcement learning methods, like RLHF, rely solely on scalar rewards, discarding rich textual rationale and leading to inefficient exploration and persistent failure patterns.

Directly integrating language feedback into RL training suffers from a paradox: intra-sample feedback causes memorization due to information leakage, while inter-sample feedback leads to "behavior collapse" as models ignore irrelevant context.
Large Language Models' unique ability to process and generate language is underutilized in RL for accelerating learning from past trials.
results: LANPO achieved an absolute improvement of up to 4.59% in zero-shot accuracy for Qwen2.5-7B and 2.64% for Qwen3-14B over the GRPO baseline on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

Training dynamics showed faster convergence and higher final accuracy; intra-sample feedback increased policy entropy (broader exploration), while inter-sample feedback decreased it (focused exploration).

Ablation studies confirmed that relevance filtering is crucial to prevent "behavior collapse" and that an optimal feedback ratio of 0.75 yielded the best performance without compromising training stability.
solution: LANPO introduces "Reward-Agnostic Reflection" to allow models to self-critique their own attempts without ground truth, mitigating information leakage.

"Relevant Abstraction" addresses behavior collapse by filtering semantically similar trajectories, summarizing them into transferable principles, and explicitly instructing the LLM to engage with this abstracted guidance.

A modular training pipeline employs an "Experience Pool" for storing distilled knowledge and an initial Supervised Fine-Tuning phase to equip the model with the necessary "atomic capabilities" for processing language feedback.
paper_summary.summary: The LANPO framework enhances reinforcement learning for large language models by integrating both linguistic and numerical feedback. This approach prevents information leakage and behavior collapse, leading to improved sample efficiency and up to 4.59% absolute higher zero-shot accuracy on mathematical reasoning benchmarks compared to baselines.

title: LANPO: Bootstrapping Language and Numerical Feedback for Reinforcement Learning in LLMs

abstract: Although pre-trained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models exhibit impressive generalization in robotic manipulation, post-training remains crucial to ensure reliable performance during deployment. However, standard offline Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) suffers from distribution shifts and catastrophic forgetting of pre-trained capabilities, while online Reinforcement Learning (RL) struggles with sparse rewards and poor sample efficiency. In this paper, we propose On-Policy VLA Distillation (VLA-OPD), a framework bridging the efficiency of SFT with the robustness of RL. Instead of relying on sparse environmental rewards, VLA-OPD leverages an expert teacher to provide dense, token-level supervision on the student's self-generated trajectories. This enables active error correction on policy-induced states while preserving pre-trained general capabilities through gentle alignment. Crucially, we formulate VLA-OPD via a Reverse-KL objective. Unlike standard Forward-KL that induces mode-covering entropy explosion, or Hard-CE that causes premature entropy collapse, our bounded mode-seeking objective ensures stable policy learning by filtering out the teacher's epistemic uncertainty while maintaining action diversity. Experiments on LIBERO and RoboTwin2.0 benchmarks demonstrate that VLA-OPD significantly improves sample efficiency over RL and robustness over SFT, while effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting during post-training.

keyInsights: On-policy distillation with dense teacher supervision effectively bridges the gap between offline SFT's efficiency and online RL's robustness for VLA models.

The Reverse-KL divergence is a superior objective for policy distillation, enabling stable learning by filtering teacher's epistemic uncertainty ("mode-seeking") while preserving action diversity, avoiding "entropy explosion" or "premature entropy collapse."
Anchoring gradient updates to the student's actively explored policy manifold (on-policy formulation) effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting, preserving generalist capabilities.
originalProblem: Offline Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) for VLA models suffers from distribution shift and catastrophic forgetting due to its off-policy nature and static datasets.
Online Reinforcement Learning (RL) for VLA models faces challenges with sparse rewards and poor sample efficiency, making it computationally prohibitive for billion-parameter models.
Existing methods that adapt SFT to on-policy settings use suboptimal distillation objectives (e.g., Forward-KL leading to "entropy explosion" or Hard-CE causing "premature entropy collapse").
results: Achieved superior sample efficiency, reaching over 90% success on LIBERO-Object in 10 steps and a 3x speedup on LIBERO-Long compared to the GRPO baseline.
Significantly boosted policy efficacy, increasing average success rates from 48.9% (1-traj SFT) to 87.4% on LIBERO and from 45.2% to 71.1% on RoboTwin2.0, nearly matching expert teacher performance.
Effectively mitigated catastrophic forgetting, maintaining competitive performance on unseen tasks while mastering target tasks, unlike offline SFT which suffered severe forgetting.
solution: An iterative, closed-loop framework (VLA-OPD) that combines on-policy trajectory sampling by the student with dense teacher labeling and mode-seeking optimization.
Leverages a robust, frozen teacher policy to provide dense, token-level action logits for every state visited by the student, replacing sparse environmental rewards.
Optimizes the student policy using a policy-gradient method with a Reverse Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence objective to ensure stable, mode-seeking policy updates.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at HKUST (GZ) developed VLA-OPD, a framework that combines offline supervised fine-tuning and online reinforcement learning for Vision-Language-Action models through on-policy distillation. It utilizes a Reverse-KL divergence objective to provide dense, token-level supervision from an expert teacher on student-generated trajectories, leading to improved sample efficiency (e.g., 3x faster convergence on LIBERO-Long) and robust performance while mitigating catastrophic forgetting.

title: VLA-OPD: Bridging Offline SFT and Online RL for Vision-Language-Action Models via On-Policy Distillation

abstract: Reward models (RMs) play a pivotal role in aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences. Due to the difficulty of obtaining high-quality human preference annotations, distilling preferences from generative LLMs has emerged as a standard practice. However, existing approaches predominantly treat teacher models as simple binary annotators, failing to fully exploit the rich knowledge and capabilities for RM distillation. To address this, we propose RM-Distiller, a framework designed to systematically exploit the multifaceted capabilities of teacher LLMs: (1) Refinement capability, which synthesizes highly correlated response pairs to create fine-grained and contrastive signals. (2) Scoring capability, which guides the RM in capturing precise preference strength via a margin-aware optimization objective. (3) Generation capability, which incorporates the teacher's generative distribution to regularize the RM to preserve its fundamental linguistic knowledge. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RM-Distiller significantly outperforms traditional distillation methods both on RM benchmarks and reinforcement learning-based alignment, proving that exploiting multifaceted teacher capabilities is critical for effective reward modeling. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first systematic research on RM distillation from generative LLMs.

keyInsights: Systematically exploiting the multi-faceted capabilities of generative LLMs (refinement, scoring, generation) is crucial for effective and high-fidelity reward model distillation, surpassing simplistic binary classification approaches.

Generating highly contrastive preference pairs through teacher-guided refinement forces the student RM to discern fine-grained quality differences, leading to learning intrinsic quality attributes rather than superficial cues.

Capturing continuous preference strength through self-calibrated scoring and margin-aware regression provides a richer, more nuanced supervisory signal than binary labels, enabling the student RM to learn precise magnitudes of preference.
Regularizing the student reward model with the teacher's generative distribution is vital to prevent catastrophic forgetting and maintain robust linguistic knowledge during discriminative training.
originalProblem: Training reward models (RMs) for LLM alignment using Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is prohibitively expensive and time-consuming due to the high cost of human preference annotations.

Existing LLM-as-a-Judge distillation methods predominantly treat powerful generative LLMs as simple binary classifiers, failing to fully exploit their advanced reasoning, critique, and generation capabilities.
This superficial utilization can lead to student RMs that learn spurious correlations, lack fine-grained preference understanding, or suffer from catastrophic forgetting of linguistic knowledge.
results: RM-Distiller consistently outperformed traditional distillation methods on RewardBench (79.5% accuracy vs. 70.3% for baseline) and RM-Bench (61.1% vs. 55.9%), and surpassed several frontier and larger open-source RMs despite using a smaller student model.

Policy models aligned with RM-Distiller-trained RMs showed superior performance in downstream RLHF experiments across various benchmarks (e.g., AlpacaEval 2.0, FollowBench), demonstrating higher win rates and improved instruction-following.

The framework exhibited exceptional fast adaptation and data efficiency, significantly outperforming baselines on a novel Arabic language task with only 10,000 instructions and maintaining high accuracy even with a reduced dataset of 1,000 instructions.
solution: RM-Distiller employs Contrastive Refinement, where a teacher LLM diagnoses inferior responses and minimally modifies them into 'refined chosen responses', creating highly contrastive preference pairs.

It utilizes Strength-Guided Preference Modeling with self-calibrated scoring, where the teacher LLM assigns continuous quality scores, and the student RM is optimized via margin-aware regression to learn preference magnitudes.

Generative Regularization is incorporated by supervising the student RM's language model head with a combination of negative log-likelihood and KL divergence loss, aligning its distribution with the teacher's to preserve linguistic proficiency.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Harbin Institute of Technology developed RM-Distiller, a framework that systematically distills generative large language models (LLMs) into reward models by leveraging their refinement, scoring, and generation capabilities. This approach achieves superior reward model performance, with average accuracies of 79.5% on RewardBench and 61.1% on RM-Bench, and leads to enhanced downstream RLHF efficacy.

title: RM-Distiller: Exploiting Generative LLM for Reward Model Distillation

abstract:
Alignment techniques enable Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate outputs
that align with human preferences and play a crucial role in their
effectiveness. However, their impact often diminishes when applied to Small
Language Models (SLMs), likely due to the limited capacity of these models.
Instead of directly applying existing alignment techniques to SLMs, we propose
to utilize a well-aligned teacher LLM to guide the alignment process for these
models, thereby facilitating the transfer of the teacher's knowledge of human
preferences to the student model. To achieve this, we first explore a
straightforward approach, Dual-Constrained Knowledge Distillation (DCKD), that
employs knowledge distillation with two KL-divergence constraints from the
aligned teacher to the unaligned student. To further enhance the student's
ability to distinguish between preferred and dispreferred responses, we then
propose Advantage-Guided Distillation for Preference Alignment (ADPA), which
leverages an advantage function from the aligned teacher to deliver more
nuanced, distribution-level reward signals for the student's alignment. Our
experimental results show that these two approaches appreciably improve the
alignment of SLMs and narrow the performance gap with larger counterparts.
Among them, ADPA demonstrates superior performance and achieves even greater
effectiveness when integrated with DCKD. Our code is available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Distilling knowledge from both preferred and dispreferred responses is crucial for effectively transferring nuanced preference alignment to SLMs.

A fine-grained, distribution-level advantage function, derived from the contrast between a DPO-aligned teacher and a reference teacher, provides stable and efficient token-level guidance for preference learning.
Combining broad knowledge transfer (via DCKD) with targeted, fine-grained preference optimization (via ADPA) yields the most robust and performant alignment for small language models.
originalProblem: Small Language Models (SLMs) experience an "alignment tax," where applying traditional preference alignment methods like RLHF degrades their general performance and generalization due to limited capacity.

Existing knowledge distillation (KD) techniques primarily focus on pre-training or instruction-tuning, failing to effectively transfer nuanced preference alignment, particularly from dispreferred responses.
The computational and memory demands of large, preference-aligned LLMs make their deployment impractical in resource-constrained environments.
results: DCKD and ADPA consistently outperformed traditional preference alignment baselines and other knowledge distillation methods on MT-Bench and AlpacaEval across various SLM sizes.

ADPA+ achieved the highest performance, demonstrating strong alignment and generalization capabilities, significantly narrowing the "alignment gap" for SLMs (e.g., LLaMA-3.2-1B on DPO-MIX-7K achieved an MT-Bench score of 4.02 with ADPA+ compared to 3.40 with DPO).
Ablation studies confirmed the critical roles of the DPO-trained teacher, dispreferred responses in DCKD, and the reference teacher in ADPA for effective preference alignment.
solution: A teacher-student framework is proposed, leveraging a large, preference-aligned LLM as the teacher to transfer alignment knowledge to an SLM.
Dual-Constrained Knowledge Distillation (DCKD) is introduced, distilling knowledge from both preferred and dispreferred responses using KL-divergence constraints.

Advantage-Guided Distillation for Preference Alignment (ADPA) is developed, which uses an advantage function derived from a DPO teacher and a reference teacher to provide fine-grained, token-level reward signals.
ADPA+, a hybrid method combining DCKD initialization with subsequent ADPA training, is used to achieve robust alignment.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Sun Yat-sen University and Meta AI developed Advantage-Guided Distillation for Preference Alignment (ADPA) and Dual-Constrained Knowledge Distillation (DCKD) to effectively align small language models (SLMs) with human preferences. Their ADPA+ method, which combines both approaches, improved LLaMA-3.2-1B's MT-Bench score to 4.02 from 3.40 (using DPO), demonstrating superior alignment and generalization while overcoming the "alignment tax" typically seen in SLMs.

title: Advantage-Guided Distillation for Preference Alignment in Small Language  Models

abstract: Recent advances have demonstrated the effectiveness of Reinforcement Learning (RL) in improving the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing works inevitably rely on high-quality instructions and verifiable rewards for effective training, both of which are often difficult to obtain in specialized domains. In this paper, we propose Self-play Reinforcement Learning (SeRL) to bootstrap LLM training with limited initial data. Specifically, SeRL comprises two complementary modules: self-instruction and self-rewarding. The former module generates additional instructions based on the available data at each training step, employing robust online filtering strategies to ensure instruction quality, diversity, and difficulty. The latter module introduces a simple yet effective majority-voting mechanism to estimate response rewards for additional instructions, eliminating the need for external annotations. Finally, SeRL performs conventional RL based on the generated data, facilitating iterative self-play learning. Extensive experiments on various reasoning benchmarks and across different LLM backbones demonstrate that the proposed SeRL yields results superior to its counterparts and achieves performance on par with those obtained by high-quality data with verifiable rewards. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: A dual-end clipped difficulty filter is critical for preventing "reward hacking," ensuring that the model trains on problems of appropriate difficulty (neither too easy nor too hard) for stable and meaningful learning.

The majority-voting mechanism for reward estimation provides significantly more stable and accurate reward signals (0.75-0.85 cosine similarity with ground-truth) compared to model-based reward approaches.

Iterative self-play with adaptive instruction generation and reliable self-rewarding enables LLMs to achieve strong reasoning performance, matching or exceeding models trained on comprehensive ground-truth datasets, even with minimal initial data.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning (RL) for Large Language Models (LLMs) typically requires vast, high-quality supervised datasets with meticulous human-labeled ground-truth for reward computation.
Acquiring extensive labeled data in specialized domains (e.g., clinical diagnostics, aerospace) is costly, time-consuming, requires expert knowledge, and is difficult to scale.

Existing self-instruction methods, while useful for supervised fine-tuning, struggle in online RL due to static data generation and unreliable reward signals when model-generated responses are used as ground-truth.
results: SeRL matches and frequently surpasses the performance of RL-GT (trained on the full MATH dataset with ground-truth rewards), with LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct outperforming RL-GT after two rounds and Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct after three rounds on several benchmarks.
The framework consistently outperforms other multi-round iterative baselines like Self-Rewarding DPO and Iterative RPO, which showed performance degradation or poor results on smaller models.

SeRL demonstrates strong generalization across various mathematical reasoning tasks, general reasoning benchmarks (MMLU-Pro), and in the medical domain (MedQA, PubMedQA, NephSAP), achieving comparable performance to RL-GT.
solution: SeRL introduces an iterative self-play RL framework that integrates a Self-Instruction module and a Self-Rewarding module within a standard RL training loop.

The Self-Instruction module dynamically generates new, diverse, and appropriately difficult instructions, adapting to the model's evolving capabilities through a robust online filtering strategy (e.g., ROUGE-L similarity, keywords, length, and difficulty filters).

The Self-Rewarding module estimates rewards without external labels using a majority-voting mechanism, where the LLM samples multiple responses for an instruction and assigns a binary reward based on agreement with the majority answer.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed SeRL, a self-play reinforcement learning framework, enabling large language models (LLMs) to enhance reasoning capabilities using limited initial data by autonomously generating instructions and estimating rewards. The framework achieves performance comparable to, and often surpasses, LLMs trained on extensive ground-truth datasets across various reasoning and medical benchmarks, demonstrating robust generalization.

title: SeRL: Self-Play Reinforcement Learning for Large Language Models with Limited Data

abstract:
In the field of large language models (LLMs), Knowledge Distillation (KD) is
a critical technique for transferring capabilities from teacher models to
student models. However, existing KD methods face limitations and challenges in
distillation of LLMs, including efficiency and insufficient measurement
capabilities of traditional KL divergence. It is shown that LLMs can serve as
an implicit reward function, which we define as a supplement to KL divergence.
In this work, we propose Direct Preference Knowledge Distillation (DPKD) for
LLMs. DPKD utilizes distribution divergence to represent the preference loss
and implicit reward function. We re-formulate KD of LLMs into two stages: first
optimizing and objective consisting of implicit reward and reverse KL
divergence and then improving the preference probability of teacher outputs
over student outputs. We conducted experiments and analysis on various datasets
with LLM parameters ranging from 120M to 13B and demonstrate the broad
applicability and effectiveness of our DPKD approach. Meanwhile, we prove the
value and effectiveness of the introduced implicit reward and output preference
in KD through experiments and theoretical analysis. The DPKD method outperforms
the baseline method in both output response precision and exact match
percentage. Code and data are available at this https URL

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: LLMs can act as implicit reward functions, and optimizing a combination of implicit reward and reverse KL divergence transforms into minimizing KLD between the student and an optimal distribution.

The DPKD objective has a strong theoretical link to the Q-function in reinforcement learning and Markov Decision Processes, suggesting that LLMs can be viewed as implicitly optimizing Q-functions during generation.

The implicit reward function acts as a crucial gradient weight, amplifying corrections for erroneous student outputs and decreasing as performance improves, guiding the model towards an optimal state.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) incur high computational costs for training, fine-tuning, and inference, making efficiency techniques like Knowledge Distillation crucial.
Traditional Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence is often insufficient for effective knowledge transfer in LLMs, struggling with complex distributions and lacking direct alignment with output quality.
Existing KD methods do not fully leverage the potential of LLMs to act as implicit reward functions for guiding distillation, especially for nuanced generation tasks.
results: DPKD consistently achieved the highest Rouge-L scores across all tested student model sizes (120M, 340M, 1.3B) and datasets (DollyEval, SelfInst, S-NI), often matching or closely approaching the teacher's performance.
GPT-4 qualitative evaluations confirmed that DPKD-generated texts are closer to reference responses than other baselines, indicating improved instruction following and generation quality.

Ablation studies showed that both the language modeling loss and length normalization are critical components, contributing positively to overall performance and preventing issues like overly short responses.
solution: DPKD re-forms white-box LLM knowledge distillation into a two-stage process, integrating an implicit reward function and reverse KL divergence optimization.
It uses a Bradley-Terry model-based preference objective, explicitly training the student model to assign higher probability to the teacher's outputs over its own, with length normalization.
The approach incorporates an additional language modeling loss to preserve general NLP capabilities and prevent catastrophic forgetting, combining it with the preference-based distillation loss.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Tsinghua University, Microsoft Research, and The Chinese University of Hong Kong developed Direct Preference Knowledge Distillation (DPKD), a method that reformulates LLM distillation to leverage an implicit reward function and direct preference learning. DPKD consistently achieved the highest Rouge-L scores across various student model sizes and datasets, demonstrating superior knowledge transfer compared to traditional methods.

title: Direct Preference Knowledge Distillation for Large Language Models

abstract: On-policy distillation is a promising approach for transferring knowledge between language models, where a student learns from dense token-level signals along its own trajectories. This framework typically uses reverse KL divergence, encouraging the student to match the teacher's high-confidence predictions. However, we show that the mode-seeking property of reverse KL reduces generation diversity and yields unstable learning signals when the teacher distribution has high entropy. To address this, we introduce Entropy-Aware On-Policy Distillation. Our key idea is augmenting the standard reverse KL objective with forward KL when teacher entropy is high, capturing the full range of plausible outputs while retaining precise imitation elsewhere. It balances mode-seeking precision with mode-covering robustness without sacrificing on-policy training efficiency. Experiments show that our method maintains generation diversity (sustained token-level entropy) and improves student-teacher alignment (lower forward KL on high-entropy tokens). Across six math reasoning benchmarks, this yields Pass@8 accuracy gains of +1.37 for Qwen3-0.6B-Base, +2.39 for Qwen3-1.7B-Base, and +5.05 for Qwen3-4B-Base compared to baseline on-policy distillation methods. These results demonstrate that accounting for teacher uncertainty is essential for maintaining diversity and achieving effective knowledge transfer.

keyInsights: Reverse KL divergence, common in on-policy distillation, is mode-seeking and causes student models to suffer from diversity degradation and unstable learning with high-entropy teacher distributions.

Dynamically applying forward KL divergence only in high-entropy regions of the teacher's output helps preserve the teacher's uncertainty and diverse modes without sacrificing on-policy training efficiency.

Preserving the teacher's token-level uncertainty in student models, especially at ambiguous decision points, directly contributes to improved reasoning capabilities and broader generalization across tasks.
originalProblem: Standard on-policy distillation (OPD) relies on reverse KL divergence, a mode-seeking objective that reduces student model generation diversity, particularly in high-entropy teacher regions.
The mode-seeking nature of reverse KL leads to unstable learning signals when the teacher's distribution has high entropy, hindering the student's ability to learn from nuanced predictions.

Existing methods struggle to balance the efficiency of on-policy training with the need to preserve the teacher's full distributional structure and uncertainty without incurring high computational costs.
results: EOPD consistently improved mathematical reasoning performance, achieving average gains of +1.16 to +1.80 in Avg@8 and +1.37 to +5.05 in Pass@8 across Qwen3 student models compared to standard OPD.
On out-of-domain benchmarks like GPQA-Diamond and MMLU-Pro, EOPD showed stable performance increases over baseline OPD, KD, and GRPO, demonstrating enhanced generalization.

Token-level entropy analysis revealed that EOPD-trained students retained substantially more probability mass in high-entropy regions (entropy ">= 1.0) compared to OPD, effectively preserving teacher uncertainty and mitigating mode collapse.
solution: EOPD dynamically combines a standard clipped reverse KL loss with forward KL divergence, which is a mode-covering objective.
The forward KL divergence component is activated only when the teacher's token-level entropy exceeds a predefined threshold, indicating teacher uncertainty.

Forward KL is approximated by summing over the teacher's top-k tokens for computational efficiency, preventing the student from learning from low-probability tails while covering significant probability mass.
paper_summary.summary: Entropy-Aware On-Policy Distillation (EOPD) is presented, a method for language model knowledge distillation that dynamically incorporates forward KL divergence based on teacher token-level entropy. EOPD consistently increased mathematical reasoning performance, showing up to a +5.05 gain in Pass@8 on benchmarks, and improved out-of-domain generalization by better preserving the teacher's uncertainty.

title: Entropy-Aware On-Policy Distillation of Language Models

abstract: On-policy distillation is pivotal for transferring reasoning capabilities to capacity-constrained models, yet remains prone to instability and negative transfer. We show that on-policy distillation can be interpreted, both theoretically and empirically, as a form of policy optimization, where the teacher-student log-likelihood ratio acts as a token reward. From this insight, we introduce REOPOLD (Relaxed On-Policy Distillation) a framework that stabilizes optimization by relaxing the strict imitation constraints of standard on-policy distillation. Specifically, REOPOLD temperately and selectively leverages rewards from the teacher through mixture-based reward clipping, entropy-based token-level dynamic sampling, and a unified exploration-to-refinement training strategy. Empirically, REOPOLD surpasses its baselines with superior sample efficiency during training and enhanced test-time scaling at inference, across mathematical, visual, and agentic tool-use reasoning tasks. Specifically, REOPOLD outperforms recent RL approaches achieving 6.7~12x greater sample efficiency and enables a 7B student to match a 32B teacher in visual reasoning with a ~3.32x inference speedup.

keyInsights: On-policy distillation is theoretically equivalent to an on-policy policy-gradient method, allowing the application of advanced RL optimization techniques.
Heavy-tailed negative rewards and near-zero rewards from the log-likelihood ratio are key bottlenecks causing instability and inefficiency in vanilla on-policy distillation.
Dynamically adjusting learning signals through reward clipping and entropy-guided sampling, combined with multi-stage training, can stabilize and enhance distillation for SLMs.
originalProblem: Small Language Models (SLMs) struggle to acquire complex reasoning abilities due to limited representational capacity and the ineffectiveness of traditional reinforcement learning with sparse rewards.
Existing on-policy distillation methods, while promising for transferring reasoning, suffer from instability, negative transfer, and rapid entropy collapse leading to premature convergence.

On-policy distillation lacks advanced optimization techniques common in modern RL, and its token-level reward signals can be either destructively heavy-tailed or negligibly near-zero, leading to inefficient learning.
results: Achieved a >6.7x sample efficiency gain on the AIME-25 benchmark compared to ProRL and >12x efficiency over other baselines in mathematical reasoning.
Demonstrated superior overall performance for 3B and 7B Qwen2.5-VL models across various visual reasoning benchmarks, also enabling up to 3.32x inference speedup.
Outperformed vanilla RKL and GRPO on four multi-modal agentic reasoning benchmarks (Pixel-Reasoner, V-Star, InfographicVQA, TallyQA) with stronger generalization and better sample efficiency.
solution: REOPOLD (Relaxed On-Policy Distillation) interprets on-policy distillation as a policy optimization problem, where the teacher-student log-likelihood ratio functions as a token-level reward, and applies a stop-gradient operator to reduce gradient variance.
It implements reward clipping via mixture-based regularization to mitigate heavy-tailed negative rewards by introducing a principled clipping threshold.

An entropy-guided token-level dynamic sampling mechanism addresses inefficiency by focusing learning on high-entropy tokens, while a multi-stage training strategy balances exploration during early phases with refinement towards high-quality trajectories later.
paper_summary.summary: REOPOLD, a framework developed for Small Language Models, stabilizes and enhances on-policy distillation by re-interpreting it as policy optimization, addressing issues like instability and entropy collapse. It achieves up to 12x sample efficiency gains in mathematical reasoning, demonstrates superior performance in visual and agentic tasks, and provides up to 3.32x inference speedup in visual reasoning.

title: Scaling Reasoning Efficiently via Relaxed On-Policy Distillation

abstract: Recently, reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has been widely used for enhancing the reasoning abilities of large language models (LLMs). A core challenge in RLVR involves managing the exchange between entropy and performance of policies. Despite the importance of this exchange, a fine-grained understanding of when and how this exchange operates most effectively remains limited. To bridge this gap, we conduct a systematic empirical analysis of the entropy-performance exchange mechanism of RLVR across different levels of granularity. Specifically, we first divide the training process into two distinct stages based on entropy dynamics, i.e., rising stage and plateau stage, and then systematically investigate how this mechanism varies across stage-level, instance-level, and token-level granularitiess. Our analysis reveals that, in the rising stage, entropy reduction in negative samples facilitates the learning of effective reasoning patterns, which in turn drives rapid performance gains. Moreover, in the plateau stage, learning efficiency strongly correlates with high-entropy tokens present in low-perplexity samples and those located at the end of sequences. Motivated by these findings, we propose two methods that dynamically adjust the reward signal using perplexity and positional information to focus RL updates on tokens that exhibit high learning potential, achieving improvements compared to the baseline methods on various LLMs.

keyInsights: Performance gains in the early 'rising stage' of RLVR are primarily driven by entropy reduction in negative samples, pruning incorrect reasoning paths.
In the later 'plateau stage,' learning shifts to a small subset of high-entropy, high-gradient tokens, typically formal reasoning and metacognitive elements, indicating refinement of nuanced logic.

Token entropy exhibits a U-shaped distribution across sequences; initial high-entropy tokens govern exploration, while terminal high-entropy tokens reflect uncertainty in final decision-making, with later positions offering more efficient learning signals.
originalProblem: Existing research on the entropy-performance exchange in RLVR for LLMs lacked a fine-grained, mechanistic understanding of its dynamics.
The influence of policy entropy on LLM performance was largely viewed at a coarse granularity, overlooking variations across training stages, individual instances, or specific tokens.
A deeper understanding was needed to develop more efficient and targeted RLVR optimization strategies for complex reasoning tasks.
results: The proposed GRPO+PPL and GRPO+POSITION methods achieved average performance improvements of 1.51% for Qwen2.5-7B and 2.31% for Qwen2.5-Math-7B over the GRPO baseline on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
Both methods sustained higher policy entropy during later training stages compared to the baseline, indicating better preservation of exploratory capabilities.
The improved models generated longer responses with increased formal reasoning and logical structuring tokens, suggesting deeper reasoning processes facilitated by the targeted advantage shaping.
solution: The study employed a systematic empirical analysis of RLVR training dynamics, using GRPO as the core framework and Qwen2.5 models on a mathematical reasoning dataset.
They introduced three novel token-level metrics: Entropy, Gradient (contribution to policy updates), and Performance Impact (causal influence on accuracy) to enable fine-grained analysis.
Two advantage shaping methods, PPL-based and Position-based, were proposed to dynamically re-weight token-level advantages based on empirical findings.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Gaoling School of Artificial Intelligence, Renmin University of China, systematically decomposed the entropy-performance exchange in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) for Large Language Models (LLMs). Their analysis revealed distinct entropy dynamics across training stages, sample quality, and token positions, leading to two advantage shaping techniques that improved mathematical reasoning performance by an average of 1.51% to 2.31% on Qwen2.5 models.

title: Decomposing the Entropy-Performance Exchange: The Missing Keys to Unlocking Effective Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, vanilla RLVR suffers from inefficient exploration, particularly when confronting "hard samples" that yield nearzero success rates. In such scenarios, the reliance on sparse outcome rewards typically results in zero-advantage estimates, effectively starving the model of supervision signals despite the high informational value of these instances. To address this, we propose P^2O, a novel framework that synergizes Prompt Optimization with Policy Optimization. P^2O identifies hard samples during training iterations and leverages the GeneticPareto (GEPA) prompt optimization algorithm to evolve prompt templates that guide the model toward discovering successful trajectories. Crucially, unlike traditional prompt engineering methods that rely on input augmentation, P^2O distills the reasoning gains induced by these optimized prompts directly into the model parameters. This mechanism provides denser positive supervision signals for hard samples and accelerates convergence. Extensive experiments demonstrate that P^2O not only achieves superior performance on in-distribution datasets but also exhibits strong generalization, yielding substantial improvements on out-of-distribution benchmarks (+4.7% avg.).
title: P^2O: Joint Policy and Prompt Optimization

abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is a promising paradigm for enhancing the reasoning ability in Large Language Models (LLMs). However, prevailing methods primarily rely on self-exploration or a single off-policy teacher to elicit long chain-of-thought (LongCoT) reasoning, which may introduce intrinsic model biases and restrict exploration, ultimately limiting reasoning diversity and performance. Drawing inspiration from multi-teacher strategies in knowledge distillation, we introduce Adaptive Multi-Guidance Policy Optimization (AMPO), a novel framework that adaptively leverages guidance from multiple proficient teacher models, but only when the on-policy model fails to generate correct solutions. This "guidance-on-demand" approach expands exploration while preserving the value of self-discovery. Moreover, AMPO incorporates a comprehension-based selection mechanism, prompting the student to learn from the reasoning paths that it is most likely to comprehend, thus balancing broad exploration with effective exploitation. Extensive experiments show AMPO substantially outperforms a strong baseline (GRPO), with a 4.3% improvement on mathematical reasoning tasks and 12.2% on out-of-distribution tasks, while significantly boosting Pass@k performance and enabling more diverse exploration. Notably, using four peer-sized teachers, our method achieves comparable results to approaches that leverage a single, more powerful teacher (e.g., DeepSeek-R1) with more data. These results demonstrate a more efficient and scalable path to superior reasoning and generalizability. Our code is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Leveraging diverse guidance from multiple teachers is more effective for enhancing LLM reasoning and generalization than relying on a single, powerful teacher.
Adaptive, 'guidance-on-demand' intervention, where external support is provided only upon student failure, maintains a balance between self-discovery and learning from external knowledge.
Selecting teacher guidance based on the student's 'comprehension' (likelihood of generating the correct answer given the teacher's path) optimizes the assimilation of new reasoning strategies.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) suffer from confined exploration, struggling to acquire new knowledge.
Complex reasoning problems often lead to sparse rewards, destabilizing training and hindering advanced skill acquisition in RLVR.
Existing mixed-policy RL methods typically rely on a single teacher, limiting exploratory diversity and leading to a narrow set of learned reasoning strategies.
results: AMPO improved average in-distribution performance by 4.3% over GRPO and 5.3% over SFT, and out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization by 6.2% over LUFFY, on Qwen2.5-7B-Ins benchmarks.
The framework achieved performance comparable to LUFFY, a strong single-teacher baseline, using over five times less data (8.5k vs. 46k samples), demonstrating enhanced data efficiency.
AMPO maintained significantly higher policy entropy throughout training and sustained validation score improvements, indicating robust exploration and preventing collapse into narrow optima.
solution: AMPO extends Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with an Adaptive Multi-Guidance Replacement mechanism, conditionally replacing student failures with diverse teacher responses from a Multi-Guidance Pool.

A Comprehension-based Guidance Selection strategy uses the student model's probability of generating the correct answer, given a teacher's reasoning path, as a proxy for selecting the most suitable guidance.
Policy optimization uses a mixed objective with sequence-level loss aggregation for off-policy guidance, ensuring each teacher response contributes equally regardless of length.
paper_summary.summary: Adaptive Multi-Guidance Policy Optimization (AMPO) enhances large language model reasoning by integrating diverse knowledge from multiple teacher models through an adaptive, comprehension-based guidance selection. The framework significantly improves generalization on out-of-distribution tasks and achieves data efficiency, outperforming existing single-teacher methods.

title: More Than One Teacher: Adaptive Multi-Guidance Policy Optimization for Diverse Exploration

abstract: Large language models trained with reinforcement learning (RL) for mathematical reasoning face a fundamental challenge: on problems the model cannot solve at all - "cliff" prompts - the RL gradient vanishes entirely, preventing any learning signal from reaching these failure modes. We introduce Hybrid Distillation Policy Optimization (HDPO), which augments standard RL with privileged self-distillation targeting cliff prompts. On each training step, HDPO identifies prompts where all rollouts fail, generates privileged rollouts by providing the model with ground-truth information, filters for correct solutions, and distills the teacher's token-level distribution into the student. Because teacher and student share the same weights - differing only in their input - the realizability gap is provably bounded, unlike cross-model distillation. We prove that R=1 filtered privileged generation recovers the optimal KL-regularized RL policy in the hard-threshold limit. Experiments on OpenMathInstruct-2 with Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B-Instruct show that HDPO consistently improves coverage metrics (pass@4 by +0.8-1.1%, pass@8 by +0.4-1.7%) while maintaining greedy accuracy, with the distillation weight lambda providing direct control over the exploration-exploitation tradeoff.

keyInsights: Same-model privileged distillation achieves a strictly tighter realizability gap compared to cross-model distillation, as demonstrated by a theoretical bound that eliminates model-mismatch terms.

Filtering privileged generations for only correct solutions (R=1) theoretically recovers the optimal KL-regularized RL policy in a hard-threshold limit, providing a principled target for distillation.
The distillation weight parameter (λ) in HDPO explicitly controls the exploration–exploitation trade-off, allowing for tuning between broader solution coverage and greedy accuracy.
originalProblem: Policy gradient methods for training large language models (LLMs) in mathematical reasoning suffer from the "cliff problem," where all generated rollouts for difficult problems yield zero reward.
The absence of a learning signal (vanishing gradients) on these unsolvable problems prevents models from improving at the boundaries of their capabilities.
Existing solutions often introduce significant complexity, requiring curriculum learning, external hint strategies, experience replay, or auxiliary reward models.
results: HDPO consistently improved solution coverage metrics, achieving up to a +1.7% increase in pass@8 (highest at 0.8398 with frozen teacher, λ=0.1) and +1.1% in pass@4 (highest at 0.7861 with drifting teacher, λ=0.01) over the GRPO baseline (0.8228 and 0.7749 respectively) on OpenMathInstruct-2.

A lower distillation weight (λ=0.01) allowed HDPO to maintain pass@1 (greedy accuracy) comparable to the baseline, while a higher weight (λ=0.1) led to a trade-off, with pass@1 decreasing by 2.3% to 2.8% for increased coverage.
The observed improvements and trade-offs were qualitatively consistent across different hardware configurations (H200 and H100 GPUs), suggesting the robustness of the method.
solution: HDPO integrates a privileged self-distillation mechanism into a standard reinforcement learning framework, specifically Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), to create a hybrid training objective.

On "cliff prompts" (problems where all standard rollouts fail), the model acts as its own teacher, generating "privileged rollouts" by conditioning on the problem augmented with its ground-truth solution.
Only correct privileged rollouts (reward=1) are filtered and used to compute a Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) distillation loss, providing a non-zero learning signal where the RL gradient vanishes.
paper_summary.summary: HDPO, a Hybrid Distillation Policy Optimization framework, addresses the "cliff problem" in mathematical reasoning for large language models by integrating reinforcement learning with a privileged self-distillation mechanism. It enables models to learn from problems they cannot initially solve, demonstrating improved solution coverage (pass@k) on benchmarks while providing a controllable trade-off with greedy accuracy (pass@1).

title: HDPO: Hybrid Distillation Policy Optimization via Privileged Self-Distillation

abstract:
Aligning small language models (SLMs) with human values typically involves
distilling preference knowledge from large language models (LLMs). However,
existing distillation methods model preference knowledge in teacher LLMs by
comparing pairwise responses, overlooking the extent of difference between
responses. This limitation hinders student SLMs from capturing the nuanced
preferences for multiple responses. In this paper, we propose a
Preference-Aligned Distillation (PAD) framework, which models teacher's
preference knowledge as a probability distribution over all potential
preferences, thereby providing more nuanced supervisory signals. Our insight in
developing PAD is rooted in the demonstration that language models can serve as
reward functions, reflecting their intrinsic preferences. Based on this, PAD
comprises three key steps: (1) sampling diverse responses using
high-temperature; (2) computing rewards for both teacher and student to
construct their intrinsic preference; and (3) training the student's intrinsic
preference distribution to align with the teacher's. Experiments on four
mainstream alignment benchmarks demonstrate that PAD consistently and
significantly outperforms existing approaches, achieving over 20\% improvement
on AlpacaEval 2 and Arena-Hard, indicating superior alignment with human
preferences. Notably, on MT-Bench, using the \textsc{Gemma} model family, the
student trained by PAD surpasses its teacher, further validating the
effectiveness of our PAD.

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: Language model preferences can be more richly modeled as a probability distribution over all possible response rankings, capturing the degree of preference and confidence.
Multiple-Choice Question (MCQ) selection probabilities can serve as a robust calibration technique for intrinsic rewards derived from language models, improving preference signal reliability.

An iterative distillation process, coupled with a preference decomposition strategy, allows for the efficient and effective transfer of nuanced preference knowledge from large teacher models to smaller student models.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) are computationally expensive, limiting their deployment in resource-constrained environments.
Small language models (SLMs) struggle to capture and align with human values and preferences, unlike larger models that benefit from extensive alignment techniques.

Traditional knowledge distillation methods for SLMs often only capture pairwise preferences or rely on complex external reward models, failing to efficiently transfer the full spectrum of a teacher model's nuanced preference knowledge.
results: The Preference-Aligned Distillation (PAD) framework significantly improved the student model's alignment on AlpacaEval 2.0, increasing its win rate from 39.51% to 49.62%.
PAD raised the student model's win rate on the Arena-Hard benchmark from 37.55% to 57.83%.

The framework consistently outperformed baseline methods such as DPO, SimPO, and PRO across multiple benchmarks, including MT-Bench and GSM8K, demonstrating improved performance over iterations and with increased sample size.
solution: The Preference-Aligned Distillation (PAD) framework captures nuanced preferences by modeling them as a probability distribution over all possible response rankings.
It incorporates a novel calibration technique that uses Multiple-Choice Question (MCQ) selection probabilities from the teacher model to derive more reliable rewards for student responses.

The framework employs an iterative distillation process, a preference decomposing strategy to reduce computational costs, and a balancing parameter (α) to control the blend of student's native and teacher's distilled preferences.
paper_summary.summary: The Preference-Aligned Distillation (PAD) framework enables smaller language models to better align with human values by modeling preferences as a probability distribution over response rankings. The method calibrates rewards using Multiple-Choice Question selection probabilities and iteratively distills knowledge from larger models, improving the win rate on AlpacaEval 2.0 from 39.51% to 49.62% and on Arena-Hard from 37.55% to 57.83%.

title: Capturing Nuanced Preferences: Preference-Aligned Distillation for Small  Language Models

abstract: Many existing studies have achieved significant improvements in the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) through reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), while the enhancement of reasoning abilities in small language models (SLMs) has not yet been sufficiently explored. Combining distilled data from larger models with RLVR on small models themselves is a natural approach, but it still faces various challenges and issues. Therefore, we propose \textit{\underline{R}}ecall-\textit{\underline{E}}xtend \textit{\underline{D}}ynamics(RED): Enhancing Small Language Models through Controlled Exploration and Refined Offline Integration. In this paper, we explore the perspective of varying exploration spaces, balancing offline distillation with online reinforcement learning. Simultaneously, we specifically design and optimize for the insertion problem within offline data. By monitoring the ratio of entropy changes in the model concerning offline and online data, we regulate the weight of offline-SFT, thereby addressing the issues of insufficient exploration space in small models and the redundancy and complexity during the distillation process. Furthermore, to tackle the distribution discrepancies between offline data and the current policy, we design a sample-accuracy-based policy shift mechanism that dynamically chooses between imitating offline distilled data and learning from its own policy.

keyInsights: Effectively enhancing SLMs requires a dynamic balance between expanding reasoning boundaries (SFT's 'Extend' phase) and optimizing existing reasoning paths (RL's 'Recall' phase), controlled by entropy changes.
Naive integration of offline distilled data into RL for SLMs leads to issues like entropy collapse or performance degradation; an accuracy-aware policy shift is necessary for robust integration.
Unified training paradigms that intelligently combine offline SFT and online RL can outperform sequential or stage-wise methods for improving SLM reasoning capabilities.
originalProblem: The application of advanced reasoning techniques like Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) to Small Language Models (SLMs, typically < 7B parameters) has been largely unexplored.
Existing SLM enhancement methods, primarily Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) distillation, often lead to 'overthinking' and 'redundant generation' issues.
Directly applying the 'pre-SFT + post-RL' pipeline common for larger models proves inefficient for SLMs due to excessively long initial responses and prolonged training cycles.
results: RED (ALL) achieved the highest overall accuracy of 41.6% across mathematical reasoning benchmarks, outperforming all baseline methods including SFT, GRPO, SFT+GRPO, and other unified/stage-wise training paradigms.

The framework demonstrated improved reasoning efficiency, achieving an average response length of 2506 tokens, which is shorter and more efficient than SFT+GRPO (3468 tokens) while maintaining higher accuracy.

Ablation studies confirmed that both Dynamic Entropy Regulation and Accuracy-aware Policy Shifts synergistically contribute to the performance gains, with their combination leading to a substantial improvement over individual components.
solution: The Recall-Extend Dynamics (RED) framework integrates SFT and RL, conceptualizing them as 'Extend' (knowledge expansion) and 'Recall' (policy refinement) phases.
Dynamic Entropy Regulation balances SFT and RL contributions by adjusting SFT weight based on the ratio of entropy changes, controlling exploration space.
An Accuracy-aware Policy Shift mechanism adaptively integrates offline distilled data by dynamically estimating off-policy probability based on problem correctness, preventing entropy collapse.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tianjin University and Hefei University of Technology developed Recall-Extend Dynamics (RED), a framework to enhance small language models (SLMs) for mathematical reasoning. This approach effectively combines offline Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with online Reinforcement Learning (RL) to achieve 41.6% overall accuracy on mathematical benchmarks while improving reasoning efficiency.

title: Recall-Extend Dynamics: Enhancing Small Language Models through Controlled Exploration and Refined Offline Integration

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training has recently driven major gains in long chain-of-thought reasoning large language models (LLMs), but the high inference cost of such models motivates distillation into smaller students. Most existing knowledge distillation (KD) methods are designed for supervised fine-tuning (SFT), relying on fixed teacher traces or teacher-student Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence-based regularization. When combined with RL, these approaches often suffer from distribution mismatch and objective interference: teacher supervision may not align with the student's evolving rollout distribution, and the KL regularizer can compete with reward maximization and require careful loss balancing. To address these issues, we propose RL-aware distillation (RLAD), which performs selective imitation during RL -- guiding the student toward the teacher only when it improves the current policy update. Our core component, Trust Region Ratio Distillation (TRRD), replaces the teacher-student KL regularizer with a PPO/GRPO-style likelihood-ratio objective anchored to a teacher--old-policy mixture, yielding advantage-aware, trust-region-bounded distillation on student rollouts and naturally balancing exploration, exploitation, and imitation. Across diverse logic reasoning and math benchmarks, RLAD consistently outperforms offline distillation, standard GRPO, and KL-based on-policy teacher-student knowledge distillation.

keyInsights: Integrating teacher guidance directly into the policy update ratio, rather than as a separate KL regularization term, prevents objective interference between reward maximization and imitation, leading to more stable training.

The 'mixture anchor' used in TRRD provides a principled way to control the update step, acting as a trust region that effectively bounds the KL divergence to a combination of previous student and teacher policies.

TRRD enables 'selective imitation' by weighting teacher influence with advantage estimates, allowing the student to prioritize learning from the teacher only when it aligns with or benefits the reward-driven policy improvement.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) trained with reinforcement learning (RL) achieve high performance but incur prohibitive inference costs, making widespread deployment challenging.
Existing knowledge distillation (KD) methods struggle with the dynamic policy shifts and potential objective interference when integrated into the RL post-training loop for LLMs.
Traditional KD approaches often suffer from distribution mismatch and can lead to unstable training or suboptimal teacher guidance in dynamic RL environments.
results: RLAD improved average accuracy on logical reasoning tasks (K&K Logistics) by up to 18% for a Qwen3-0.6B student model compared to GRPO, with larger gains on harder problem subsets.

On complex math reasoning benchmarks (MATH500, AMC, AIME), RLAD achieved an average score improvement of up to 5.5 points for a Qwen3-8B-Base student over GRPO, demonstrating significantly more stable training dynamics than KDRL.
The method incurred only approximately 12% additional training latency compared to vanilla GRPO, with no extra inference-time cost, making it an efficient solution for distilling RL-trained LLMs.
solution: Introduced RL-Aware Distillation (RLAD), a framework designed to seamlessly integrate teacher guidance into the reinforcement learning optimization process for LLMs.

Developed Trust Region Ratio Distillation (TRRD) as the core component, which modifies the policy update ratio of RL algorithms (like GRPO) to include a 'mixture anchor' blending the old student policy and the teacher policy.
TRRD applies teacher influence in an advantage-aware and trust-region bounded manner, ensuring selective imitation where guidance is proportional to the reward signal and within stable update bounds.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from AWS Agentic AI developed Reinforcement-aware Distillation (RLAD), a framework that integrates teacher guidance into the Reinforcement Learning (RL) post-training process for Large Language Models (LLMs) via Trust Region Ratio Distillation (TRRD). RLAD consistently improved reasoning task performance, achieving up to 18% higher accuracy in logical reasoning and a 5.5 average score increase in math reasoning, while demonstrating superior training stability and minimal computational overhead compared to existing methods.

title: Reinforcement-aware Knowledge Distillation for LLM Reasoning

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a standard paradigm for refining large language models (LLMs) beyond pre-training and instruction tuning. A prominent line of work is RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR), which leverages automatically verifiable outcomes (e.g., correctness or executability) to generate reward signals. While efficient, this framework faces two key limitations: First, its binary feedback is too sparse to capture the quality of the reasoning process. Second, its coarse-grained rewards potentially lead to vanishing gradients. Inspired by observations from human learning, we introduce a RL technique that integrates verifiable outcomes with the model's own confidence estimates. This joint design enriches the reward signal, providing finer-grained feedback and implicitly supervising the reasoning process. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method enhances RL performance across multiple datasets and reduces token consumption during inference, while incurring negligible additional training cost. Moreover, it can be used as a plug-in module to enhance other state-of-the-art RL methods.

keyInsights: Model confidence in its output can serve as a proxy for the quality and soundness of its internal reasoning process, similar to human learning.
Integrating confidence with objective verifiable outcomes provides a finer-grained and more informative reward signal than sparse binary rewards.
A simple clipping mechanism is essential to stabilize confidence-weighted rewards within group-relative policy optimization, preventing catastrophic training collapse by ensuring bounded rewards.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) in LLMs provides sparse, binary feedback, failing to assess the quality of the reasoning process leading to an answer.
The coarse granularity of binary rewards can cause vanishing gradients and inefficient learning, especially when all model outputs are uniformly correct or incorrect.
Reliance solely on LLM-generated confidence for intrinsic rewards can be unreliable due to miscalibration.
results: ConfClip consistently improved accuracy across various LLM sizes (Qwen2.5-3B, Qwen2.5-7B) and datasets, including MATH (up to 72.36%), GSM8K (up to 85.22%), and a significant gain on AIME24 (from 1.77% to 5.21% for 3B model).
The method reduces token consumption during inference, enabling models to achieve higher accuracy with more concise outputs, indicating suppression of "overthinking" on mastered problems.

ConfClip maintained training stability and was successfully integrated as a plug-in module with state-of-the-art RL algorithms like GSPO, with ablation studies confirming the critical role of the clipping mechanism in preventing training collapse.
solution: ConfClip reweights standard correctness rewards by the geometric mean of token probabilities for the entire generated response, capturing the model's confidence.
It introduces a penalty for incorrect answers, setting the reward to negative confidence to explicitly differentiate and penalize highly confident wrong predictions.
A clipping mechanism is applied to the confidence-weighted rewards to prevent training instability, ensuring rewards remain bounded and do not inadvertently promote low-confidence incorrectness.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University and Ant Group developed ConfClip, a reward mechanism for reinforcement learning in large language models that integrates model confidence with verifiable outcomes. This method enhanced LLM performance, increasing MATH accuracy by 1.27% on Qwen2.5-3B and GSM8K by 2.96% on Qwen2.5-7B, while also reducing inference token consumption and maintaining training stability.

title: ConfClip: Confidence-Weighted and Clipped Reward for Reinforcement Learning in LLMs

abstract: In modern large language models (LLMs), LLM alignment is of crucial importance and is typically achieved through methods such as reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and direct preference optimization (DPO). However, in most existing methods for LLM alignment, all tokens in the response are optimized using a sparse, response-level reward or preference annotation. The ignorance of token-level rewards may erroneously punish high-quality tokens or encourage low-quality tokens, resulting in suboptimal performance and slow convergence speed. To address this issue, we propose AlignDistil, an RLHF-equivalent distillation method for token-level reward optimization. Specifically, we introduce the reward learned by DPO into the RLHF objective and theoretically prove the equivalence between this objective and a token-level distillation process, where the teacher distribution linearly combines the logits from the DPO model and a reference model. On this basis, we further bridge the accuracy gap between the reward from the DPO model and the pure reward model, by building a contrastive DPO reward with a normal and a reverse DPO model. Moreover, to avoid under- and over-optimization on different tokens, we design a token adaptive logit extrapolation mechanism to construct an appropriate teacher distribution for each token. Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our AlignDistil over existing methods and showcase fast convergence due to its token-level distributional reward optimization.

keyInsights: The core RLHF objective can be reframed as a token-level policy distillation problem, where the policy learns to match a synthesized ideal token distribution from DPO and reference models.
Enhancing the DPO reward with a contrastive approach using both preferred and 'dispreferred' (reverse DPO) examples significantly improves reward accuracy and generalization.

Adaptive weighting for token-level extrapolation, based on the divergence between DPO and reverse DPO distributions, allows for balanced optimization, precisely guiding the policy where it matters most.
originalProblem: Most existing LLM alignment methods like RLHF and DPO rely on sparse, response-level rewards or preferences, overlooking the quality of individual tokens.
Coarse-grained feedback leads to suboptimal performance because high-quality tokens can be erroneously punished or low-quality tokens encouraged within a sequence.
The sparse nature of feedback limits the learning signal, resulting in inefficient optimization and slow convergence speeds for LLM alignment.
results: AlignDistil consistently outperforms leading LLM alignment methods, including DPO and RTO, on AlpacaEval 2.0 (e.g., achieving 19.45% length-controlled win rate with Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct vs. DPO's 14.35%).
The method demonstrates more than 2x faster convergence speed compared to token-level scalar reward optimization and drastically faster rates than sentence-level methods.
Off-policy AlignDistil achieves comparable performance to its on-policy counterpart, offering a more efficient alignment solution by avoiding costly online data generation.
solution: The research proves that the RLHF objective, when using a DPO reward, is theoretically equivalent to a token-level policy distillation process.
A contrastive DPO reward is introduced, leveraging both normal and reverse DPO models to generate a more accurate and robust reward signal for the distillation.

A token adaptive logit extrapolation mechanism is developed to dynamically adjust the distillation strength for each token, preventing under- or over-optimization based on their criticality for preference.
paper_summary.summary: AlignDistil introduces a token-level policy distillation framework for Large Language Model alignment, reformulating the RLHF objective to optimize against fine-grained, adaptive distributional rewards. This approach significantly improves performance on standard benchmarks and achieves over 2x faster convergence compared to existing methods.

title: AlignDistil: Token-Level Language Model Alignment as Adaptive Policy Distillation

abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) are typically trained using reinforcement learning with verifiable reward (RLVR) to enhance their reasoning abilities. In this paradigm, policies are updated using both positive and negative self-generated rollouts, which correspond to distinct sample polarities. In this paper, we provide a systematic investigation into how these sample polarities affect RLVR training dynamics and behaviors. We find that positive samples sharpen existing correct reasoning patterns, while negative samples encourage exploration of new reasoning paths. We further explore how adjusting the advantage values of positive and negative samples at both the sample level and the token level affects RLVR training. Based on these insights, we propose an Adaptive and Asymmetric token-level Advantage shaping method for Policy Optimization, namely A3PO, that more precisely allocates advantage signals to key tokens across different polarities. Experiments across five reasoning benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.

keyInsights: Positive samples reinforce existing correct reasoning (sharpening), decreasing entropy and response length, while negative samples promote exploration and discovery of new paths by maintaining entropy and longer responses.
Both positive and negative samples are essential for robust RLVR training, as single-polarity training often leads to performance degradation across various LLMs.
Key tokens for exploration are low-probability tokens in positive samples and high-probability tokens in negative samples, as they help prevent premature convergence and maintain entropy.
originalProblem: Existing Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) algorithms treat positive and negative self-generated samples largely symmetrically, obscuring their distinct roles.
The precise impact of sample polarity on Large Reasoning Model (LRM) training dynamics, model behavior, and the balance between exploration and exploitation remained underexplored.
Prior limited studies on sample contributions lacked generalizability across various tasks or models, necessitating a systematic investigation.
results: A3PO consistently outperformed all baseline methods, including DAPO, across three different LLMs and five reasoning benchmarks, showing statistically significant improvements.
The method achieved an average accuracy of 40.4% on Qwen2.5-7B-Math, surpassing DAPO's 36.3%, and 48.7% on Qwen3-8B-Base compared to DAPO's 44.1%.
A3PO effectively maintains higher model entropy and longer responses during training, which contributes to improved generalization performance and mitigates training-inference mismatch.
solution: Performed a systematic empirical analysis of positive and negative samples in RLVR, evaluating their effects on model entropy, response length, reward, and generalization across three base LLMs.
Investigated the impact of advantage shaping at both polarity-level and token-level granularities, considering token entropy and probability distributions.

Proposed A3PO, an adaptive and asymmetric token-level advantage shaping method that dynamically amplifies low-probability tokens in positive samples and high-probability tokens in negative samples early in training.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Renmin University, CUHK, and Ant Group introduced A3PO, an adaptive and asymmetric token-level advantage shaping method for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). This approach consistently surpassed baseline methods like DAPO, achieving an average accuracy of 40.4% on Qwen2.5-7B-Math compared to DAPO's 36.3%, by better balancing exploitation and exploration for large reasoning models.

title: Rethinking Sample Polarity in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards

abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) succeeds in reasoning tasks (e.g., math and code) by checking the final verifiable answer (i.e., a verifiable dot signal). However, extending this paradigm to open-ended generation is challenging because there is no unambiguous ground truth. Relying on single-dot supervision often leads to inefficiency and reward hacking. To address these issues, we propose reinforcement learning with verifiable reference-based rewards (RLVRR). Instead of checking the final answer, RLVRR extracts an ordered linguistic signal from high-quality references (i.e, reward chain). Specifically, RLVRR decomposes rewards into two dimensions: content, which preserves deterministic core concepts (e.g., keywords), and style, which evaluates adherence to stylistic properties through LLM-based verification. In this way, RLVRR combines the exploratory strength of RL with the efficiency and reliability of supervised fine-tuning (SFT). Extensive experiments on more than 10 benchmarks with Qwen and Llama models confirm the advantages of our approach. RLVRR (1) substantially outperforms SFT trained with ten times more data and advanced reward models, (2) unifies the training of structured reasoning and open-ended generation, and (3) generalizes more effectively while preserving output diversity. These results establish RLVRR as a principled and efficient path toward verifiable reinforcement learning for general-purpose LLM alignment. We release our code and data at this https URL.

keyInsights: Extending the verifiable reward paradigm to open-ended generation is achievable by decomposing rewards into a structured "reward chain" of content and style elements derived from reference responses.

LLM-generated verifiers are cost-effective for offline data construction and provide precise, rule-based feedback, circumventing the need for expensive human preference data and learned reward models during RL training.

Using Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) to evaluate content keyword alignment effectively captures both presence and sequential order, which is crucial for preventing reward hacking and improving semantic fidelity.
originalProblem: Existing LLM alignment methods like RLHF and DPO are highly expensive due to reliance on human preference data and are prone to reward hacking.
Traditional Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is limited to tasks with unambiguous ground truth or a single, verifiable outcome, making it inapplicable to open-ended generation.
Simple metrics like BLEU used as rewards for open-ended tasks often fail to capture nuanced content and stylistic properties, leading to misaligned and noisy signals.
results: RLVRR consistently outperforms SFT (even with 10x more data), DPO, and various RM/GRM-based approaches across over 10 benchmarks, showing improvements of +2.3 to +2.7 points over RM.

The framework unifies training for both structured reasoning and open-ended generation tasks; a model trained on a mix of 10K math and open-ended data matches specialized performance and surpasses INSTRUCT models trained on millions of samples.

Ablation studies confirm the critical role of content reward and the superiority of LCS for alignment and hierarchical keyword extraction; the data construction cost for 10K samples is approximately $21.36, with negligible overhead during RL training (0.71%).
solution: The RLVRR framework introduces a "Reward Chain" concept, which extracts an ordered sequence of verifiable linguistic signals from high-quality reference responses rather than relying on a single verifiable dot.

Rewards are decomposed into two components: a content reward derived from hierarchically extracted keywords and their Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) alignment, and a style reward computed by executing LLM-generated verifiable Python functions.

An off-the-shelf LLM (e.g., GPT-4o-mini) is used offline to generate these verifiable content keywords and style code checks, and the final policy is optimized using GRPO with the aggregated rule-based reward signal.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Huawei Technologies Co.,Ltd developed Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reference-Based Rewards (RLVRR), a framework that leverages "reward chains" derived from reference responses to align large language models for open-ended generation tasks. RLVRR consistently outperforms SFT, DPO, and reward model-based approaches, while enabling unified training for both reasoning and open-ended tasks with significantly reduced data and computational costs.

title: From Verifiable Dot to Reward Chain: Harnessing Verifiable Reference-based Rewards for Reinforcement Learning of Open-ended Generation

abstract: Reinforcement learning has emerged as a principled post-training paradigm for Temporal Video Grounding (TVG) due to its on-policy optimization, yet existing GRPO-based methods remain fundamentally constrained by sparse reward signals and substantial computational overhead. We propose Video-OPD, an efficient post-training framework for TVG inspired by recent advances in on-policy distillation. Video-OPD optimizes trajectories sampled directly from the current policy, thereby preserving alignment between training and inference distributions, while a frontier teacher supplies dense, token-level supervision via a reverse KL divergence objective. This formulation preserves the on-policy property critical for mitigating distributional shift, while converting sparse, episode-level feedback into fine-grained, step-wise learning signals.
