Building on Video-OPD, we introduce Teacher-Validated Disagreement Focusing (TVDF), a lightweight training curriculum that iteratively prioritizes trajectories that are both teacher-reliable and maximally informative for the student, thereby improving training efficiency. Empirical results demonstrate that Video-OPD consistently outperforms GRPO while achieving substantially faster convergence and lower computational cost, establishing on-policy distillation as an effective alternative to conventional reinforcement learning for TVG.

keyInsights: Dense, token-level supervision derived from a teacher model can effectively resolve the credit assignment problem in on-policy learning for long-horizon tasks like TVG, improving sample efficiency and convergence speed.

On-policy distillation can maintain the benefits of on-policy optimization (avoiding distributional shift) while offering significant computational efficiency gains by eliminating the need for multiple rollouts inherent in traditional RL algorithms.

A lightweight curriculum, Teacher-Validated Disagreement Focusing (TVDF), which validates teacher reliability and prioritizes samples with high student-teacher divergence, further enhances optimization and performance by focusing training on informative samples.
originalProblem: Existing on-policy reinforcement learning methods like GRPO for Temporal Video Grounding (TVG) suffer from sparse, sequence-level reward signals, leading to inefficient credit assignment and slow convergence.
GRPO incurs prohibitive computational overhead due to its requirement for multiple on-policy rollouts per training sample to obtain stable reward estimates.
Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) methods face a distributional mismatch between training and inference, causing error accumulation in long-horizon tasks like TVG.
results: Video-OPD achieved state-of-the-art performance among open-source models on key Temporal Video Grounding benchmarks (Charades-TimeLens, ActivityNet-TimeLens, QVHighlights-TimeLens), with an average improvement exceeding 17% over GRPO.
The method demonstrated strong generalization capabilities, consistently yielding state-of-the-art results on broader video understanding benchmarks such as TempCompass, MVBench, and Video-MME.
Video-OPD converged substantially faster and required only approximately 20% of GRPO's training cost, showcasing a more favorable efficiency-performance trade-off.
solution: Video-OPD employs an on-policy distillation paradigm that samples trajectories from the student model and uses a fixed teacher model to evaluate the student's generated tokens.

It introduces a dense, token-level learning signal derived from the reverse KL divergence between student and teacher log-probabilities, eliminating sparse rewards and enabling precise credit assignment.
The framework requires only a single on-policy rollout per training sample, drastically reducing computational cost and accelerating convergence compared to multi-rollout GRPO.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Xiaomi Inc., Zhejiang University, and Renmin University of China developed Video-OPD, an on-policy distillation method for efficiently post-training multimodal large language models for temporal video grounding (TVG). This framework provides dense, token-level supervision, reducing computational cost by requiring only a single rollout per training instance, and achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing previous GRPO-based methods by over 17% on TVG benchmarks while converging substantially faster.

title: Video-OPD: Efficient Post-Training of Multimodal Large Language Models for Temporal Video Grounding via On-Policy Distillation

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) can align language models with non-differentiable reward signals, such as human preferences. However, a major challenge arises from the sparsity of these reward signals - typically, there is only a single reward for an entire output. This sparsity of rewards can lead to inefficient and unstable learning. To address this challenge, our paper introduces an novel framework that utilizes the critique capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to produce intermediate-step rewards during RL training. Our method involves coupling a policy model with a critic language model, which is responsible for providing comprehensive feedback of each part of the output. This feedback is then translated into token or span-level rewards that can be used to guide the RL training process. We investigate this approach under two different settings: one where the policy model is smaller and is paired with a more powerful critic model, and another where a single language model fulfills both roles. We assess our approach on three text generation tasks: sentiment control, language model detoxification, and summarization. Experimental results show that incorporating artificial intrinsic rewards significantly improve both sample efficiency and the overall performance of the policy model, supported by both automatic and human evaluation.

keyInsights: Leveraging the critique capabilities of LLMs can effectively generate accurate, fine-grained intrinsic feedback for text generation, overcoming limitations of sparse extrinsic rewards.
Dense intrinsic rewards significantly enhance the sample efficiency of RL training, allowing policy models to learn desired behaviors with fewer interactions.
The framework is versatile, enabling both separate policy-critic LLMs and self-critique where a single LLM acts as both policy and critic.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning (RL) in text generation suffers from sparse reward signals, as feedback is typically provided only after an entire text sequence is generated.
The temporal credit assignment problem makes it difficult for RL agents to identify which specific tokens or phrases contributed positively or negatively to the final outcome.
Inefficient and unstable learning results from delayed and infrequent rewards, hindering policy convergence and requiring many samples.
results: RELC consistently achieved higher performance and faster learning across sentiment control, LM detoxification, and abstractive text summarization tasks compared to PPO baselines.
In sentiment control, RELC obtained 59.06% positive continuations from negative prompts, surpassing PPO (43.13%) and other advanced baselines.
For LM detoxification, RELC reduced the maximum toxicity probability to 0.7%, a substantial improvement over PPO's 4.4%, and showed superior alignment with human preferences in summarization.
solution: Introduces RELC (Rewards from Language model Critique), a framework where a specialized critic LLM provides dense, intermediate-step intrinsic rewards.
The critic LLM evaluates policy model outputs, pinpoints specific tokens/segments contributing to quality, and translates these verbal evaluations into numerical intrinsic reward signals.

The policy model is trained to optimize a combined reward function consisting of a weighted sum of both sparse extrinsic rewards and dense intrinsic rewards, integrated with standard RL algorithms like PPO.
paper_summary.summary: A new framework, RELC (Rewards from Language model Critique), leverages large language models (LLMs) to generate dense, token- or span-level intrinsic rewards, addressing the sparse reward problem in reinforcement learning for text generation. This approach significantly enhances sample efficiency and improves performance across tasks like sentiment control, detoxification, and summarization, outperforming baseline methods and fine-grained human feedback.

title: Beyond Sparse Rewards: Enhancing Reinforcement Learning with Language Model Critique in Text Generation

abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) improves reasoning in large language models but treats all correct solutions equally, potentially reinforcing flawed traces that get correct answers by chance. We observe that better reasoning are better teachers: high-quality solutions serve as more effective demonstrations than low-quality ones. We term this teaching ability Demonstration Utility, and show that the policy model's own in-context learning ability provides an efficient way to measure it, yielding a quality signal termed Evidence Gain. To employ this signal during training, we introduce In-Context RLVR. By Bayesian analysis, we show that this objective implicitly reweights rewards by Evidence Gain, assigning higher weights to high-quality traces and lower weights to low-quality ones, without requiring costly computation or external evaluators. Experiments on mathematical benchmarks show improvements in both accuracy and reasoning quality over standard RLVR.

keyInsights: High-quality reasoning traces possess higher "Demonstration Utility," meaning they are more effective as in-context demonstrations, a property quantifiable by how much they improve a model's log-likelihood on other high-quality examples.

A Bayesian identity reveals that conditioning a policy on a demonstration implicitly reweights the policy's objective by a factor related to the exponential of the Evidence Gain, providing a cost-effective quality signal.

This implicit reweighting allows for fine-grained quality supervision during RLVR training without the need for auxiliary reward models, expensive human annotations, or explicit computation of Evidence Gain during every training step.
originalProblem: Standard Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) assigns binary rewards, failing to distinguish between high-quality and low-quality reasoning processes leading to a correct answer.
Process Reward Models (PRMs), which offer fine-grained feedback, require expensive human annotations or training auxiliary evaluators, limiting their scalability.
There is a need for a cost-effective method to enhance LLM reasoning quality within the RLVR framework without explicit, step-level supervision or significant computational overhead.
results: Evidence Gain showed positive Spearman correlations (0.27-0.45) with LLM-based reasoning quality scores across eight dimensions, confirming its ability to reflect various aspects of reasoning quality.
IC-DAPO improved average accuracy on six mathematical benchmarks by +2.5 points for the 1.5B model and achieved gains of +5.6 points on AIME24 and +5.8 points on AIME25.

Training with IC-DAPO consistently increased the mean Evidence Gain and overall reasoning quality of generated traces during training, with less than 5% additional computational overhead compared to standard RLVR.
solution: Develops "Evidence Gain" as an intrinsic quality signal, quantifying how effectively a generated reasoning trace serves as an in-context demonstration for a held-out set of high-quality reference solutions.

Proposes "In-Context RLVR," an implicit reward reweighting mechanism where a demonstration is prepended to the input during standard RL training, leveraging a Bayesian identity to effectively reweight rewards by Evidence Gain.

Integrates this approach with the DAPO algorithm, named IC-DAPO, performing standard RL updates in a demonstration-conditioned setting to promote high-quality reasoning without explicit Evidence Gain computation.
paper_summary.summary: Mei et al. introduce "Evidence Gain," an intrinsic quality signal derived from a Large Language Model's (LLM) in-context learning ability, and develop "In-Context RLVR" to implicitly integrate this signal into Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) training. This approach improved average accuracy on mathematical benchmarks by +2.5 points for the 1.5B model and by +5.6 points on challenging tasks like AIME24, without requiring costly step-level human annotations.

title: Good Reasoning Makes Good Demonstrations: Implicit Reasoning Quality Supervision via In-Context Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiers (RLVR) is a central paradigm for improving large language model (LLM) reasoning, yet existing methods often suffer from limited exploration. Policies tend to collapse onto a few reasoning patterns and prematurely stop deep exploration, while conventional entropy regularization introduces only local stochasticity and fails to induce meaningful path-level diversity, leading to weak and unstable learning signals in group-based policy optimization. We propose DSDR, a Dual-Scale Diversity Regularization reinforcement learning framework that decomposes diversity in LLM reasoning into global and coupling components. Globally, DSDR promotes diversity among correct reasoning trajectories to explore distinct solution modes. Locally, it applies a length-invariant, token-level entropy regularization restricted to correct trajectories, preventing entropy collapse within each mode while preserving correctness. The two scales are coupled through a global-to-local allocation mechanism that emphasizes local regularization for more distinctive correct trajectories. We provide theoretical support showing that DSDR preserves optimal correctness under bounded regularization, sustains informative learning signals in group-based optimization, and yields a principled global-to-local coupling rule. Experiments on multiple reasoning benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in accuracy and pass@k, highlighting the importance of dual-scale diversity for deep exploration in RLVR. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Effective exploration in LLM reasoning requires a coordinated strategy for diversity at both global (inter-mode) and local (intra-mode) scales.
Correctness-aligned diversity shaping for high-quality trajectories can maintain robust learning signals in RL, even when verifier rewards would otherwise become uniform.
A theoretically grounded global-to-local coupling mechanism can effectively guide local token-level exploration towards expanding under-explored, correct reasoning trajectories.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) trained with Reinforcement Learning with Verifiers (RLVR) suffer from limited deep exploration, where policies prematurely converge on a narrow set of reasoning patterns.
This leads to a collapse in solution diversity, hindering `pass@k` performance and generalization, especially on complex or out-of-domain tasks.

Existing diversity methods provide only local stochasticity or lack effective coordination between different scales of diversity, resulting in weak and unstable learning signals in group-based optimization when rewards saturate.
results: DSDR consistently outperforms all baselines (Backbone, GRPO, DAPO) in `Pass@1` and `Avg@16` accuracy across diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks (e.g., Qwen3-4B achieved 48.0 `Pass@1` vs. GRPO's 39.3 average).

The framework effectively expands the set of correct reasoning trajectories, demonstrated by consistent gains across `Pass@k` evaluations (for k from 2 to 64), with performance gaps widening for higher `k` values.
Ablation studies confirm the necessity and complementary roles of both global diversity and the global-to-local coupling for DSDR's improved performance and stable training dynamics.
solution: DSDR proposes a dual-scale diversity regularization approach that explicitly decomposes diversity into global (trajectory-level) and local (token-level) components.
It incorporates correctness-aligned global diversity bonuses by assigning extra credit to distinct correct solutions, ensuring informative learning signals in group-based policy optimization.

A principled global-to-local coupling mechanism adaptively allocates local entropy regularization strength based on the global distinctiveness of correct trajectories, prioritizing exploration around unique and verified reasoning paths.
paper_summary.summary: DSDR introduces a reinforcement learning framework that enhances exploration in large language model reasoning by coordinating global and local diversity regularization. The approach improves performance across mathematical reasoning benchmarks by fostering a wider array of correct solution paths and maintaining stable learning signals.

title: DSDR: Dual-Scale Diversity Regularization for Exploration in LLM Reasoning

abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has emerged as a promising paradigm for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models particularly in mathematics. Current approaches in this domain present a clear trade-off: PPO-style methods (e.g., GRPO/DAPO) offer training stability but exhibit slow learning trajectories due to their trust-region constraints on policy updates, while REINFORCE-style approaches (e.g., CISPO) demonstrate improved learning efficiency but suffer from performance instability as they clip importance sampling weights while still permitting non-zero gradients outside the trust-region. To address these limitations, we introduce DISPO, a simple yet effective REINFORCE-style algorithm that decouples the up-clipping and down-clipping of importance sampling weights for correct and incorrect responses, yielding four controllable policy update regimes. Through targeted ablations, we uncover how each regime impacts training: for correct responses, weights >1 increase the average token entropy (i.e., exploration) while weights <1 decrease it (i.e., distillation) -- both beneficial but causing gradual performance degradation when excessive. For incorrect responses, overly restrictive clipping triggers sudden performance collapse through repetitive outputs (when weights >1) or vanishing response lengths (when weights <1). By separately tuning these four clipping parameters, DISPO maintains the exploration-distillation balance while preventing catastrophic failures, achieving 61.04% on AIME'24 (vs. 55.42% CISPO and 50.21% DAPO) with similar gains across various benchmarks and models.

keyInsights: The stability-efficiency dilemma in RLVR for LLMs can be effectively resolved by providing fine-grained, differentiated control over various policy update regimes.
Exploration is primarily driven by amplified positive updates (reward > 0, IS weight > 1), while distillation is facilitated by suppressed positive updates (reward > 0, IS weight < 1).

Specific negative update regimes are crucial for stability: amplified negative updates (reward < 0, IS weight > 1) prevent 'repetition collapse,' and suppressed negative updates (reward < 0, IS weight < 1) prevent 'length collapse' by avoiding over-aggressive unlearning.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) for LLMs faces a fundamental trade-off: PPO-style methods offer stability but slow learning, while REINFORCE-style methods provide efficiency but are prone to catastrophic performance collapses.
Existing REINFORCE-style methods apply uniform clipping strategies to policy updates, failing to account for the distinct and often conflicting dynamics of different types of updates.
The root causes of instability, such as sudden performance drops or repetitive/truncated outputs, in REINFORCE-style LLM training were not fully understood.
results: DISPO consistently outperformed both PPO-style (DAPO) and REINFORCE-style (CISPO) baselines across Qwen3 models (8B, 14B, 30B MoE) on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, achieving up to 61.04% accuracy on AIME’24 with Qwen3-14B.
The decoupled clipping strategy maintained training stability and promoted a healthier exploration trajectory (increasing entropy) compared to baselines, preventing sudden performance collapses.

Ablation studies confirmed the necessity of distinct negative update regimes, showing that disabling amplified negative updates led to 'repetition collapse' and disabling suppressed negative updates caused 'length collapse,' validating the proposed mechanism for preventing instabilities.
solution: Introduces DISPO (Decoupled Importance Sampling-weighted Policy Optimization), an off-policy REINFORCE variant for RLVR that incorporates a novel decoupled clipping strategy.

Utilizes four distinct clipping parameters for importance sampling weights, conditioned on whether the reward is positive or negative, and whether the importance sampling weight is greater or less than one, creating four unique policy update regimes.
Integrates effective components from prior work such as group-relative advantage estimation, token-level normalization, dynamic sampling of responses, and an overlong response penalty term.
paper_summary.summary: DISPO, developed by researchers from Cornell University and Amazon AGI, introduces a decoupled clipping strategy for reinforcement learning, enhancing the training efficiency and stability of large language models for mathematical reasoning. This approach achieved an average accuracy of 61.04% on AIME'24 with Qwen3-14B, outperforming CISPO's 55.42% and DAPO's 50.21% while preventing common training collapses.

title: DISPO: Enhancing Training Efficiency and Stability in Reinforcement Learning for Large Language Model Mathematical Reasoning

abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a promising paradigm for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). In this context, models explore reasoning trajectories and exploit rollouts with correct answers as positive signals for policy optimization. However, these rollouts might involve flawed patterns such as answer-guessing and jump-in-reasoning. Such flawed-positive rollouts are rewarded identically to fully correct ones, causing policy models to internalize these unreliable reasoning patterns. In this work, we first conduct a systematic study of flawed-positive rollouts in RL and find that they enable rapid capability gains during the early optimization stage, while constraining reasoning capability later by reinforcing unreliable patterns. Building on these insights, we propose Flawed-Aware Policy Optimization (FAPO), which presents a parameter-free reward penalty for flawed-positive rollouts, enabling the policy to leverage them as useful shortcuts in the warm-up stage, securing stable early gains, while gradually shifting optimization toward reliable reasoning in the later refinement stage. To accurately and comprehensively detect flawed-positive rollouts, we introduce a generative reward model (GenRM) with a process-level reward that precisely localizes reasoning errors. Experiments show that FAPO is effective in broad domains, improving outcome correctness, process reliability, and training stability without increasing the token budget.
title: FAPO: Flawed-Aware Policy Optimization for Efficient and Reliable Reasoning

abstract: Reinforcement learning has emerged as a powerful paradigm for unlocking reasoning capabilities in large language models. However, relying on sparse rewards makes this process highly sample-inefficient, as models must navigate vast search spaces with minimal feedback. While classic curriculum learning aims to mitigate this by ordering data based on complexity, the right ordering for a specific model is often unclear. To address this, we propose Goldilocks, a novel teacher-driven data sampling strategy that aims to predict each question's difficulty for the student model. The teacher model selects questions of appropriate difficulty for the student model, i.e., questions that are neither too easy nor too hard (Goldilocks principle), while training the student with GRPO. By leveraging the student's performance on seen samples, the teacher continuously adapts to the student's evolving abilities. On OpenMathReasoning dataset, Goldilocks data sampling improves the performance of models trained with standard GRPO under the same compute budget.

keyInsights: The magnitude of the policy gradient in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is maximized when a question is at the 'edge of solvability,' meaning the student model's success probability is approximately 0.5.
An online-trained teacher model can accurately predict the learning potential of unseen questions by estimating the standard deviation of rewards, enabling dynamic curriculum generation.
Focusing training on tasks with 'just right' difficulty, where gradients are strongest, significantly improves sample efficiency and learning speed for LLMs on reasoning tasks.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning (RL) for fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs) on reasoning tasks suffers from sparse rewards, making learning slow and computationally expensive due to extensive exploration.
Current Curriculum Learning (CL) strategies for LLMs are impractical for large-scale training, either requiring resource-intensive history tracking or assuming pre-categorized data.

The reliance on Outcome Supervision (OS) provides feedback only for the final answer, leading to vanishing gradients when tasks are consistently too easy or too hard, thus wasting computational resources.
results: Goldilocks increased Pass@1 accuracy on the OpenMathReasoning dataset for all tested LLMs, including a 3.2 percentage point gain for Olmo2-1B (from 11.7% to 14.9%) and 2.8 percentage point gain for Qwen2.5-1.5B (from 30.6% to 33.4%).
Models trained with Goldilocks exhibited steeper learning curves, higher average training rewards, and consistently larger policy gradient norms, indicating faster and more efficient learning.

The teacher model demonstrated robust generalization by reducing Mean Absolute Error on unseen samples and dynamically adapted the curriculum, maintaining a high standard deviation of selected task difficulties.
solution: A teacher-driven Goldilocks framework trains in parallel with a student LLM, dynamically selecting optimal training examples for RL fine-tuning.
The teacher model, a trainable LLM, predicts the utility of unseen questions by estimating the student's success probability, targeting questions where this probability is near 0.5.
An epsilon-greedy data selection policy prioritizes these 'just right' questions, which offer the maximum learning signal, over uniformly random exploration.
paper_summary.summary: The Goldilocks framework introduces a teacher-driven data sampling strategy that dynamically tunes task difficulty to optimize reinforcement learning for fine-tuning large language models on reasoning tasks. This approach consistently improved Pass@1 accuracies across diverse models, such as increasing Olmo2-1B's performance from 11.7% to 14.9%, by prioritizing questions that yield the maximum learning signal.

title: Goldilocks RL: Tuning Task Difficulty to Escape Sparse Rewards for Reasoning

abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD), which samples trajectories from the student model and supervises them with a teacher at the token level, avoids relying solely on verifiable terminal rewards and can yield better generalization than off-policy distillation. However, OPD requires expensive on-the-fly sampling of the student policy during training, which substantially increases training cost, especially for long responses. Our initial analysis shows that, during OPD, training signals are often concentrated in the prefix of each output, and that even a short teacher-generated prefix can significantly help the student produce the correct answer. Motivated by these observations, we propose a simple yet effective modification of OPD: we apply the distillation objective only to prefixes of student-generated outputs and terminate each sampling early during distillation. Experiments on a suite of AI-for-Math and out-of-domain benchmarks show that on-policy prefix distillation matches the performance of full OPD while reducing training FLOP by 2x-47x.

keyInsights: Learning signals in on-policy distillation are concentrated in the prefixes of generated outputs, suggesting that early tokens are most critical for guiding correct reasoning.
Aligning the student's policy with the teacher's only for the initial prefix can be sufficient for the student to complete complex reasoning tasks correctly.
An on-policy setting with reverse KL divergence proves to be the most robust and stable configuration for distillation when applying short-prefix truncation.
originalProblem: Large language models face high computational costs, memory, and energy requirements, hindering their practical deployment.
Sequence-level knowledge distillation (SeqKD) suffers from 'exposure bias,' leading to poor generalization and error accumulation.
Traditional on-policy distillation (OPD) is computationally expensive, requiring full rollouts and teacher scoring for potentially thousands of tokens per training step.
results: OPPD consistently reduces training FLOP by 2x to 47x compared to full on-policy distillation.
OPPD achieves competitive in-domain performance on AI-for-Math benchmarks, often matching full OPD while using less than 50% of the FLOP.

The linear prefix scheduling strategy enables competitive out-of-domain performance for students of varying sizes, effectively combining the efficiency of short prefixes with the benefits of longer-horizon feedback.
solution: On-policy Prefix Distillation (OPPD) truncates student rollouts during training and applies the distillation objective only to a predefined initial prefix length (`L_train`).
A linear prefix scheduling strategy gradually increases `L_train` over training updates, allowing the student to learn from progressively longer reasoning horizons.
The method uses reverse Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence with unbiased estimation via sampling to align student and teacher policies on the supervised prefix.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Optum AI developed On-policy Prefix Distillation (OPPD), a method that applies on-policy distillation objectives only to the initial segments of LLM outputs during training. This approach reduces training computational cost by 2x to 47x while maintaining competitive performance on reasoning tasks, especially when combined with a linear prefix scheduling strategy.

title: Fast and Effective On-policy Distillation from Reasoning Prefixes

abstract: The reward model (RM), as the core component of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for large language models (LLMs), responsible for providing reward signals to generated responses. However, the mainstream discriminative reward modeling is inadequate in terms of token-level interaction, making its judgment signals vulnerable to being hacked by misallocated attention to context. This stems from two fundamental limitations: (1) Current preference modeling employs decoder-only architectures, where the unidirectional causal attention mechanism leads to forward-decaying intra-sequence attention within the prompt-response sequence. (2) The independent Siamese-encoding paradigm induces the absence of token-level inter-sequence attention between chosen and rejected sequences. To address this "attention hacking", we propose "Interaction Distillation", a novel training framework for more adequate discriminative reward modeling via attention-level optimization. The method introduces an interaction-based natural language understanding model as the teacher to provide sophisticated token interaction patterns via comprehensive attention, and guides the reward modeling to simulate teacher model's interaction pattern through an attentional alignment objective. Through extensive experiments, interaction distillation has demonstrated its ability to provide more stable and generalizable reward signals compared to state-of-the-art RM optimization methods that target data noise, highlighting the attention hacking constitute a more fundamental limitation in discriminative RM.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

title: Alleviating Attention Hacking in Discriminative Reward Modeling through Interaction Distillation

abstract: Large language models are increasingly post-trained with reinforcement learning in verifiable domains such as code and math. Yet, current methods for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) learn only from a scalar outcome reward per attempt, creating a severe credit-assignment bottleneck. Many verifiable environments actually provide rich textual feedback, such as runtime errors or judge evaluations, that explain why an attempt failed. We formalize this setting as reinforcement learning with rich feedback and introduce Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO), which converts tokenized feedback into a dense learning signal without any external teacher or explicit reward model. SDPO treats the current model conditioned on feedback as a self-teacher and distills its feedback-informed next-token predictions back into the policy. In this way, SDPO leverages the model's ability to retrospectively identify its own mistakes in-context. Across scientific reasoning, tool use, and competitive programming on LiveCodeBench v6, SDPO improves sample efficiency and final accuracy over strong RLVR baselines. Notably, SDPO also outperforms baselines in standard RLVR environments that only return scalar feedback by using successful rollouts as implicit feedback for failed attempts. Finally, applying SDPO to individual questions at test time accelerates discovery on difficult binary-reward tasks, achieving the same discovery probability as best-of-k sampling or multi-turn conversations with 3x fewer attempts.
title: Reinforcement Learning via Self-Distillation

abstract: On-policy reinforcement learning (RL) methods widely used for language model post-training, like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), often suffer from limited exploration and early saturation due to low sampling diversity. While off-policy data can help, current approaches that mix entire trajectories cause significant policy mismatch and instability. In this work, we propose the $\textbf{S}$ingle-sample Mix-p$\textbf{O}$licy $\textbf{U}$nified $\textbf{P}$aradigm (SOUP), a framework that unifies off- and on-policy learning within individual samples at the token level. It confines off-policy influence to the prefix of a generated sequence sampled from historical policies, while the continuation is generated on-policy. Through token-level importance ratios, SOUP effectively leverages off-policy information while preserving training stability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SOUP consistently outperforms standard on-policy training and existing off-policy extensions. Our further analysis clarifies how our fine-grained, single-sample mix-policy training can improve both exploration and final performance in LLM RL.

keyInsights: Confining off-policy influence to sequence prefixes while ensuring on-policy generation for continuations effectively balances exploration benefits with training stability requirements.

Token-level importance ratio modeling mitigates policy mismatch and prevents the 'sample-level clipping saturation problem,' enabling high-entropy tokens to contribute more effectively to gradient updates.
The mix-policy approach expands the explorable solution space during inference and maintains higher effective policy entropy during training by reducing clipping of crucial exploratory tokens.
originalProblem: Standard on-policy reinforcement learning for Large Language Models (LLMs) suffers from limited exploration and early saturation, leading to performance plateaus.
Existing off-policy RL methods for LLMs often introduce significant policy mismatch when mixing entire sequences, resulting in biased gradient estimates and unstable optimization.
The inability to effectively leverage diverse historical data at a fine-grained level limits LLMs' ability to discover novel, high-value reasoning paths.
results: SOUP consistently outperformed standard on-policy training (DAPO) and prior off-policy methods (LUFFY, M2PO) across six mathematical reasoning benchmarks for Qwen2.5-Math-7B and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B, achieving an average performance improvement of approximately 2.86% for Qwen2.5-Math-7B.
Training dynamics showed SOUP exhibits more stable reward curves with smaller fluctuations and smoother convergence compared to on-policy training.

Ablation studies confirmed that all non-zero off-policy ratios improved performance over pure on-policy training, with optimal performance observed at a behavior policy update frequency (T=8), and SOUP's relay sampling improved `pass@k` for larger `k` values (k 
8) at inference time.
solution: SOUP introduces a token-level single-sample mix-policy paradigm, where off-policy prefixes are sampled from historical policy checkpoints and concatenated with on-policy continuations generated by the current actor policy.

It employs fine-grained, token-level importance ratios to distinguish between off-policy prefix tokens and on-policy continuation tokens, ensuring stable gradient estimation despite mixed data sources.
The framework incorporates flexible truncation strategies (e.g., length-ratio, entropy-based) to define the off-policy prefix, allowing controlled integration of exploration data.
paper_summary.summary: The SOUP framework integrates off-policy prefixes from historical checkpoints with on-policy continuations at the token level, creating a single-sample mix-policy approach for Large Language Models. This method consistently outperforms standard on-policy and prior off-policy training on mathematical reasoning tasks, improving average performance by up to 2.86% for Qwen2.5-Math-7B while enhancing training stability.

title: SOUP: Token-level Single-sample Mix-policy Reinforcement Learning for Large Language Models

abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is a powerful paradigm to align language models with human preferences using pairwise comparisons. However, its binary win-or-loss supervision often proves insufficient for training small models with limited capacity. Prior works attempt to distill information from large teacher models using behavior cloning or KL divergence. These methods often focus on mimicking current behavior and overlook distilling reward modeling. To address this issue, we propose \textit{Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation} (TVKD), which introduces an auxiliary reward from the value function of the teacher model to provide a soft guide. This auxiliary reward is formulated to satisfy potential-based reward shaping, ensuring that the global reward structure and optimal policy of DPO are preserved. TVKD can be integrated into the standard DPO training framework and does not require additional rollouts. Our experimental results show that TVKD consistently improves performance across various benchmarks and model sizes.

organization_info.name: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology
Gwangju Institute of Science and Technology
Gwangju Institute of Science and Technology (GIST)

keyInsights: A DPO-trained policy can be interpreted as a soft-optimal solution to a token-level Markov Decision Process (MDP), allowing its soft value function to serve as a proxy for the teacher's implicit reward modeling.

Potential-based Reward Shaping (PBRS) provides a robust theoretical guarantee that auxiliary reward signals will not alter the optimal policy of the underlying MDP, preventing conflicts with the DPO objective.
Distilling the teacher's value function offers fine-grained, soft supervision that transfers a deeper understanding of 'goodness' from the teacher, going beyond simple output behavior imitation.
originalProblem: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) provides coarse, binary feedback at the sequence level, making it less effective for training small language models (SLMs) with limited capacity.

Existing preference distillation methods often mimic the teacher's output behavior rather than its underlying reward structure, which can lead to compounding errors and conflict with the primary DPO objective.
There is a need for a theoretically sound and computationally efficient mechanism to transfer nuanced preference understanding from large teacher models to smaller student models.
results: TVKD consistently outperforms various baseline distillation methods and DPO variants across major evaluation benchmarks, including RM scores, MT-Bench ratings, AlpacaEval win rates, and the Open LLM Leaderboard.
The method achieves these performance improvements in a fully offline training setting, demonstrating significant computational efficiency compared to online distillation approaches.

Empirical analysis validates the theoretical principles of PBRS, showing that state-dependent shaping maintains consistency with preference data, while action-dependent shaping leads to performance degradation and lower margin accuracy.
solution: TVKD integrates soft reward labels derived from a DPO-trained teacher model's value function into the standard DPO objective for the student model.
The teacher's value function is formulated as a state-dependent auxiliary reward, satisfying the conditions for Potential-based Reward Shaping (PBRS) to ensure policy invariance.
The method operates in a fully offline manner, pre-computing teacher logits to derive the value function, thus avoiding additional student rollouts or online interactions.
paper_summary.summary: Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation (TVKD) introduces a method for aligning small language models (SLMs) with human preferences by extracting soft reward labels from a DPO-trained teacher's value function. This approach, which applies potential-based reward shaping, consistently improves SLM performance on preference benchmarks while maintaining computational efficiency in an offline setting.

title: Preference Distillation via Value based Reinforcement Learning

abstract:
Current Reinforcement Learning (RL) methodologies for Large Language Models
(LLMs) often rely on simplistic, outcome-based reward signals (e.g., final
answer correctness), which limits the depth of learning from each interaction.
This paper introduces Socratic Reinforcement Learning (Socratic-RL), a novel,
process-oriented framework designed to address this limitation. Socratic-RL
operates on the principle that deeper understanding is achieved by reflecting
on the causal reasons for errors and successes within the reasoning process
itself. The framework employs a decoupled "Teacher-Student" architecture, where
a "Teacher AI" analyzes interaction histories, extracts causal insights, and
formulates them into structured "viewpoints." These viewpoints, acting as
distilled guidance, are then used by a "Student AI" to enhance its subsequent
reasoning. A key innovation is the iterative self-improvement of the Teacher
AI, enabling its reflective capabilities to evolve through a meta-learning
loop. To manage the accumulation of knowledge, a distillation mechanism
compresses learned viewpoints into the Student's parameters. By focusing on
process rather than just outcome, Socratic-RL presents a pathway toward
enhanced sample efficiency, superior interpretability, and a more scalable
architecture for self-improving AI systems. This paper details the foundational
concepts, formal mechanisms, synergies, challenges, and a concrete research
roadmap for this proposed framework.
title: Socratic RL: A Novel Framework for Efficient Knowledge Acquisition  through Iterative Reflection and Viewpoint Distillation

abstract: Distilling the tool-using capabilities of large language models (LLMs) into smaller, more efficient small language models (SLMs) is a key challenge for their practical application. The predominant approach, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), suffers from poor generalization as it trains models to imitate a static set of teacher trajectories rather than learn a robust methodology. While reinforcement learning (RL) offers an alternative, the standard RL using sparse rewards fails to effectively guide SLMs, causing them to struggle with inefficient exploration and adopt suboptimal strategies. To address these distinct challenges, we propose MENTOR, a framework that synergistically combines RL with teacher-guided distillation. Instead of simple imitation, MENTOR employs an RL-based process to learn a more generalizable policy through exploration. In addition, to solve the problem of reward sparsity, it uses a teacher's reference trajectory to construct a dense, composite teacher-guided reward that provides fine-grained guidance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MENTOR significantly improves the cross-domain generalization and strategic competence of SLMs compared to both SFT and standard sparse-reward RL baselines.

organization_info.name: Seoul National University of Science and Technology (SEOULTECH)

title: MENTOR: A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Enabling Tool Use in Small Models via Teacher-Optimized Rewards

abstract: Reinforcement Learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a primary learning paradigm for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of multi-modal large language models (MLLMs). However, during RL training, the enormous state space of MLLM and sparse rewards often leads to entropy collapse, policy degradation, or over-exploitation of suboptimal behaviors. This necessitates an exploration strategy that maintains productive stochasticity while avoiding the drawbacks of uncontrolled random sampling, yielding inefficient exploration. In this paper, we propose CalibRL, a hybrid-policy RLVR framework that supports controllable exploration with expert guidance, enabled by two key mechanisms. First, a distribution-aware advantage weighting scales updates by group rareness to calibrate the distribution, therefore preserving exploration. Meanwhile, the asymmetric activation function (LeakyReLU) leverages the expert knowledge as a calibration baseline to moderate overconfident updates while preserving their corrective direction. CalibRL increases policy entropy in a guided manner and clarifies the target distribution by estimating the on-policy distribution through online sampling. Updates are driven by these informative behaviors, avoiding convergence to erroneous patterns. Importantly, these designs help alleviate the distributional mismatch between the model's policy and expert trajectories, thereby achieving a more stable balance between exploration and exploitation. Extensive experiments across eight benchmarks, including both in-domain and out-of-domain settings, demonstrate consistent improvements, validating the effectiveness of our controllable hybrid-policy RLVR training. Code is available at this https URL.
title: Controllable Exploration in Hybrid-Policy RLVR for Multi-Modal Reasoning

abstract: The push to compress and impart the proficiency of Large Language Models (LLMs) into more deployable and efficient Small Language Models (SLMs) has benefited from improvements in knowledge distillation (KD) techniques. These techniques allow a smaller student model to learn from a more capable and larger teacher model's responses. However, distillation often revolves around the student model merely copying the teacher's in-distribution responses, limiting its generalisability. This limitation is amplified on reasoning tasks and can be computationally expensive. In this study, we propose AdvDistill, a reward-guided dataset distillation framework. We utilise multiple generations (responses) from a teacher for each prompt and assign rewards based on rule-based verifiers. These varying and normally distributed rewards serve as weights when training student models. Our methods and their subsequent behavioural analysis demonstrate a significant improvement in student model performance for mathematical and complex reasoning tasks, showcasing the efficacy and benefits of incorporating a rewarding mechanism in dataset distillation processes.
title: Enhancing Reasoning Capabilities in SLMs with Reward Guided Dataset Distillation

abstract: Long context reasoning in large language models (LLMs) has demonstrated enhancement of their cognitive capabilities via chain-of-thought (CoT) inference. Training such models is usually done via reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) in reasoning based problems, like math and programming. However, RLVR is limited by several bottlenecks, such as, lack of dense reward, and inadequate sample efficiency. As a result, it requires significant compute resources in post-training phase. To overcome these limitations, in this work, we propose \textbf{Semantic Soft Bootstrapping (SSB)}, a self-distillation technique, in which the same base language model plays the role of both teacher and student, but receives different semantic contexts about the correctness of its outcome at training time. The model is first prompted with a math problem and several rollouts are generated. From them, the correct and most common incorrect response are filtered, and then provided to the model in context to produce a more robust, step-by-step explanation with a verified final answer. This pipeline automatically curates a paired teacher-student training set from raw problem-answer data, without any human intervention. This generation process also produces a sequence of logits, which is what the student model tries to match in the training phase just from the bare question alone. In our experiment, Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct on GSM8K dataset via parameter-efficient fine-tuning. We then tested its accuracy on MATH500, and AIME2024 benchmarks. Our experiments show a jump of 10.6%, and 10% improvements in accuracy, respectively, over group relative policy optimization (GRPO), which is a commonly used RLVR algorithm. Our code is available at this https URL, and the model, curated dataset is available at this https URL.
title: Semantic Soft Bootstrapping: Long Context Reasoning in LLMs without Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Large language models are increasingly post-trained with reinforcement learning in verifiable domains such as code and math. Yet, current methods for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) learn only from a scalar outcome reward per attempt, creating a severe credit-assignment bottleneck. Many verifiable environments actually provide rich textual feedback, such as runtime errors or judge evaluations, that explain why an attempt failed. We formalize this setting as reinforcement learning with rich feedback and introduce Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO), which converts tokenized feedback into a dense learning signal without any external teacher or explicit reward model. SDPO treats the current model conditioned on feedback as a self-teacher and distills its feedback-informed next-token predictions back into the policy. In this way, SDPO leverages the model's ability to retrospectively identify its own mistakes in-context. Across scientific reasoning, tool use, and competitive programming on LiveCodeBench v6, SDPO improves sample efficiency and final accuracy over strong RLVR baselines. Notably, SDPO also outperforms baselines in standard RLVR environments that only return scalar feedback by using successful rollouts as implicit feedback for failed attempts. Finally, applying SDPO to individual questions at test time accelerates discovery on difficult binary-reward tasks, achieving the same discovery probability as best-of-k sampling or multi-turn conversations with 3x fewer attempts.

title: Reinforcement Learning via Self-Distillation

abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is a powerful paradigm to align language models with human preferences using pairwise comparisons. However, its binary win-or-loss supervision often proves insufficient for training small models with limited capacity. Prior works attempt to distill information from large teacher models using behavior cloning or KL divergence. These methods often focus on mimicking current behavior and overlook distilling reward modeling. To address this issue, we propose \textit{Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation} (TVKD), which introduces an auxiliary reward from the value function of the teacher model to provide a soft guide. This auxiliary reward is formulated to satisfy potential-based reward shaping, ensuring that the global reward structure and optimal policy of DPO are preserved. TVKD can be integrated into the standard DPO training framework and does not require additional rollouts. Our experimental results show that TVKD consistently improves performance across various benchmarks and model sizes.

title: Preference Distillation via Value based Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Knowledge distillation improves large language model (LLM) reasoning by compressing the knowledge of a teacher LLM to train smaller LLMs. On-policy distillation advances this approach by having the student sample its own trajectories while a teacher LLM provides dense token-level supervision, addressing the distribution mismatch between training and inference in off-policy distillation methods. However, on-policy distillation typically requires a separate, often larger, teacher LLM and does not explicitly leverage ground-truth solutions available in reasoning datasets. Inspired by the intuition that a sufficiently capable LLM can rationalize external privileged reasoning traces and teach its weaker self (i.e., the version without access to privileged information), we introduce On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), a framework where a single model acts as both teacher and student by conditioning on different contexts. The teacher policy conditions on privileged information (e.g., verified reasoning traces) while the student policy sees only the question; training minimizes the per-token divergence between these distributions over the student's own rollouts. We demonstrate the efficacy of our method on multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks, achieving 4-8x token efficiency compared to reinforcement learning methods such as GRPO and superior performance over off-policy distillation methods.
title: Self-Distilled Reasoner: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Large Language Models

abstract:
Knowledge distillation is used, in generative language modeling, to train a
smaller student model using the help of a larger teacher model, resulting in
improved capabilities for the student model. In this paper, we formulate a more
general framework for knowledge distillation where the student learns from the
teacher during training, and also learns to ask for the teacher's help at
test-time following rules specifying test-time restrictions. Towards this, we
first formulate knowledge distillation as an entropy-regularized value
optimization problem. Adopting Path Consistency Learning to solve this, leads
to a new knowledge distillation algorithm using on-policy and off-policy
demonstrations. We extend this using constrained reinforcement learning to a
framework that incorporates the use of the teacher model as a test-time
reference, within constraints. In this situation, akin to a human learner, the
model needs to learn not only the learning material, but also the relative
difficulty of different sections to prioritize for seeking teacher help. We
examine the efficacy of our method through experiments in translation and
summarization tasks, observing trends in accuracy and teacher use, noting that
our approach unlocks operating points not available to the popular Speculative
Decoding approach.
title: Knowledge Distillation with Training Wheels

abstract: Reinforcement learning in large language models (LLMs) often relies on scalar rewards, a practice that discards valuable textual rationale buried in the rollouts, forcing the model to explore \textit{de novo} with each attempt and hindering sample efficiency. While LLMs can uniquely learn from language feedback provided in-context, naively integrating on-line experiences into RL training presents a paradox: feedback from the same problem risks information leakage and memorization, while feedback from different problems often leads to behavior collapse due to irrelevant context. To resolve this tension, we propose \textbf{Language-And-Numerical Policy Optimization (LANPO)}, a framework that cleanly separates the roles of feedback: language guides exploration, while numerical rewards drive optimization. LANPO builds a dynamic experience pool from past trials and introduces two principles to ensure feedback is effective: \emph{Reward-Agnostic Reflection} for safe intra-sample self-correction and \emph{Relevant Abstraction} to distill generalizable lessons from inter-sample experiences. Across mathematical reasoning benchmarks, LANPO enables 7B and 14B models to significantly outperform strong baselines trained with GRPO in test accuracy. Our work provides a robust method for integrating historical experiences into the LLM RL loop, creating more effective and data-efficient learning agents.
title: LANPO: Bootstrapping Language and Numerical Feedback for Reinforcement Learning in LLMs

abstract: In modern large language models (LLMs), LLM alignment is of crucial importance and is typically achieved through methods such as reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and direct preference optimization (DPO). However, in most existing methods for LLM alignment, all tokens in the response are optimized using a sparse, response-level reward or preference annotation. The ignorance of token-level rewards may erroneously punish high-quality tokens or encourage low-quality tokens, resulting in suboptimal performance and slow convergence speed. To address this issue, we propose AlignDistil, an RLHF-equivalent distillation method for token-level reward optimization. Specifically, we introduce the reward learned by DPO into the RLHF objective and theoretically prove the equivalence between this objective and a token-level distillation process, where the teacher distribution linearly combines the logits from the DPO model and a reference model. On this basis, we further bridge the accuracy gap between the reward from the DPO model and the pure reward model, by building a contrastive DPO reward with a normal and a reverse DPO model. Moreover, to avoid under- and over-optimization on different tokens, we design a token adaptive logit extrapolation mechanism to construct an appropriate teacher distribution for each token. Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our AlignDistil over existing methods and showcase fast convergence due to its token-level distributional reward optimization.
title: AlignDistil: Token-Level Language Model Alignment as Adaptive Policy Distillation

title: Reinforcement Learning via Self-Distillation

title: Reinforcement Learning via Self-Distillation

abstract: The success of RL for LLM post-training stems from an unreasonably uninformative source: a single bit of information per rollout as binary reward or preference label. At the other extreme, distillation offers dense supervision but requires demonstrations, which are costly and difficult to scale. We study text feedback as an intermediate signal: richer than scalar rewards, yet cheaper than complete demonstrations. Textual feedback is a natural mode of human interaction and is already abundant in many real-world settings, where users, annotators, and automated judges routinely critique LLM outputs. Towards leveraging text feedback at scale, we formalize a multi-turn RL setup, RL from Text Feedback (RLTF), where text feedback is available during training but not at inference. Therefore, models must learn to internalize the feedback in order to improve their test-time single-turn performance. To do this, we propose two methods: Self Distillation (RLTF-SD), which trains the single-turn policy to match its own feedback-conditioned second-turn generations; and Feedback Modeling (RLTF-FM), which predicts the feedback as an auxiliary objective. We provide theoretical analysis on both methods, and empirically evaluate on reasoning puzzles, competition math, and creative writing tasks. Our results show that both methods consistently outperform strong baselines across benchmarks, highlighting the potential of RL with an additional source of rich supervision at scale.
title: Expanding the Capabilities of Reinforcement Learning via Text Feedback

title: Self-Distilled Reasoner: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Large Language Models

abstract: Training-time privileged information (PI) can enable language models to succeed on tasks they would otherwise fail, making it a powerful tool for reinforcement learning in hard, long-horizon settings. However, transferring capabilities learned with PI to policies that must act without it at inference time remains a fundamental challenge. We study this problem in the context of distilling frontier models for multi-turn agentic environments, which typically hide their internal reasoning and expose only action trajectories. This breaks standard distillation pipelines, since successful behavior is observable, but the reasoning process is not. For this, we introduce {\pi}-Distill, a joint teacher-student objective that trains a PI-conditioned teacher and an unconditioned student simultaneously using the same model. Additionally, we also introduce On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), an alternative approach that trains using Reinforcement Learning (RL) with a reverse KL-penalty between the student and the PI-conditioned teacher. We show that both of these algorithms effectively distill frontier agents using action-only PI. Specifically, we find that {\pi}-Distill and, in some cases, OPSD, outperform industry standard practices (Supervised finetuning followed by RL) that assume access to full Chain-of-Thought supervision across multiple agentic benchmarks, models, and forms of PI. We complement our results with extensive analysis that characterizes the factors enabling effective learning with PI, focusing primarily on {\pi}-Distill and characterizing when OPSD is competitive.
title: Privileged Information Distillation for Language Models

title: LANPO: Bootstrapping Language and Numerical Feedback for Reinforcement Learning in LLMs

abstract: Recent advances in large language model (LLM) post-training have leveraged two distinct paradigms to enhance reasoning capabilities: reinforcement learning (RL) and knowledge distillation (KD). While RL enables the emergence of complex reasoning behaviors, it often suffers from low sample efficiency when the initial policy struggles to explore high-reward trajectories. Conversely, KD improves learning efficiency via mimicking the teacher model but tends to generalize poorly to out-of-domain scenarios. In this work, we present \textbf{KDRL}, a \textit{unified post-training framework} that jointly optimizes a reasoning model through teacher supervision (KD) and self-exploration (RL). Specifically, KDRL leverages policy gradient optimization to simultaneously minimize the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence (RKL) between the student and teacher distributions while maximizing the expected rule-based rewards. We first formulate a unified objective that integrates GRPO and KD, and systematically explore how different KL approximations, KL coefficients, and reward-guided KD strategies affect the overall post-training dynamics and performance. Empirical results on multiple reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that KDRL outperforms GRPO and various KD baselines while achieving a favorable balance between performance and reasoning token efficiency. These findings indicate that integrating KD and RL serves as an effective and efficient strategy to train reasoning LLMs.
title: KDRL: Post-Training Reasoning LLMs via Unified Knowledge Distillation and Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Recent advances have demonstrated the effectiveness of Reinforcement Learning (RL) in improving the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing works inevitably rely on high-quality instructions and verifiable rewards for effective training, both of which are often difficult to obtain in specialized domains. In this paper, we propose Self-play Reinforcement Learning (SeRL) to bootstrap LLM training with limited initial data. Specifically, SeRL comprises two complementary modules: self-instruction and self-rewarding. The former module generates additional instructions based on the available data at each training step, employing robust online filtering strategies to ensure instruction quality, diversity, and difficulty. The latter module introduces a simple yet effective majority-voting mechanism to estimate response rewards for additional instructions, eliminating the need for external annotations. Finally, SeRL performs conventional RL based on the generated data, facilitating iterative self-play learning. Extensive experiments on various reasoning benchmarks and across different LLM backbones demonstrate that the proposed SeRL yields results superior to its counterparts and achieves performance on par with those obtained by high-quality data with verifiable rewards. Our code is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Hangzhou High-Tech Zone (Binjiang) Institute of Blockchain and Data Security

keyInsights: Iterative self-instruction combined with robust online filtering, especially a dual-end clipped difficulty filter, is crucial for generating high-quality, diverse data and preventing reward hacking in unsupervised LLM-RL.

A majority-voting mechanism for reward estimation provides a more accurate and stable signal for LLMs in data-scarce settings compared to model-based reward methods, enabling effective training without explicit ground truth.

The SeRL framework successfully allows LLMs to achieve reasoning performance on par with or superior to ground-truth supervised RL, demonstrating that significant gains are possible with limited initial data and self-generated rewards.
originalProblem: Applying Reinforcement Learning (RL) to enhance Large Language Models (LLMs) requires extensive, high-quality, and verifiable datasets, which are costly and difficult to acquire in specialized domains.
Existing self-instruction methods for LLMs often produce static or noisy data, leading to unreliable reward signals and hindering effective online RL training.
Traditional self-rewarding approaches frequently rely on inaccurate model-based evaluations or predefined rules, particularly with smaller models, limiting their utility in unsupervised RL.
results: SeRL with LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct consistently outperformed RL-GT (Reinforcement Learning with Ground Truth) across most math benchmarks after two iterations, showing continued improvement.
The dual-end clipped difficulty filter was critical in preventing "reward hacking," where models exhibited high average rewards but plummeting test set accuracy, ensuring meaningful learning signals.
The majority-voting self-rewarding mechanism demonstrated significantly higher alignment (cosine similarity of 0.75-0.85) with ground-truth rewards compared to model-based methods (0.17-0.49).
solution: A Self-Instruction module dynamically generates diverse and appropriately difficult training instructions by sampling from both a small seed dataset and previously generated data, adapting to the LLM's evolving capabilities.
An Online Instruction Filter maintains data quality and diversity through similarity, keyword, length, and a crucial dual-end clipped difficulty filter to prevent reward hacking.

A Self-Rewarding module employs a majority-voting mechanism over multiple sampled responses to estimate reliable binary rewards, converting Pass@K performance into a Pass@1 signal for unsupervised RL training.
paper_summary.summary: SeRL enables Reinforcement Learning for Large Language Models using limited initial data by dynamically generating diverse instructions and estimating rewards through a majority-voting mechanism. The framework achieves performance comparable to RL with extensive ground-truth data, extending LLM reasoning capabilities to specialized, data-scarce domains.

title: SeRL: Self-Play Reinforcement Learning for Large Language Models with Limited Data

title: Reinforcement Learning via Self-Distillation

abstract: Context distillation enables language models to internalize in-context knowledge into their parameters. In our work, we propose On-Policy Context Distillation (OPCD), a framework that bridges on-policy distillation with context distillation by training a student model on its own generated trajectories while minimizing reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence against a context-conditioned teacher. We demonstrate the effectiveness of OPCD on two important applications: experiential knowledge distillation, where models extract and consolidate transferable knowledge from their historical solution traces, and system prompt distillation, where models internalize beneficial behaviors encoded in optimized prompts. Across mathematical reasoning, text-based games, and domain-specific tasks, OPCD consistently outperforms baseline methods, achieving higher task accuracy while better preserving out-of-distribution capabilities. We further show that OPCD enables effective cross-size distillation, where smaller student models can internalize experiential knowledge from larger teachers.
title: On-Policy Context Distillation for Language Models

abstract: Many existing studies have achieved significant improvements in the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) through reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), while the enhancement of reasoning abilities in small language models (SLMs) has not yet been sufficiently explored. Combining distilled data from larger models with RLVR on small models themselves is a natural approach, but it still faces various challenges and issues. Therefore, we propose \textit{\underline{R}}ecall-\textit{\underline{E}}xtend \textit{\underline{D}}ynamics(RED): Enhancing Small Language Models through Controlled Exploration and Refined Offline Integration. In this paper, we explore the perspective of varying exploration spaces, balancing offline distillation with online reinforcement learning. Simultaneously, we specifically design and optimize for the insertion problem within offline data. By monitoring the ratio of entropy changes in the model concerning offline and online data, we regulate the weight of offline-SFT, thereby addressing the issues of insufficient exploration space in small models and the redundancy and complexity during the distillation process. Furthermore, to tackle the distribution discrepancies between offline data and the current policy, we design a sample-accuracy-based policy shift mechanism that dynamically chooses between imitating offline distilled data and learning from its own policy.
title: Recall-Extend Dynamics: Enhancing Small Language Models through Controlled Exploration and Refined Offline Integration
